metadata.source: Verbatim book FAQs + book-derived concept/technique FAQs (Cayoun & Grabovac 2nd Ed.) + verbatim app/assessment help docs from app.mindfulness.net.au + curated special-population FAQs

faq.question: What is unilateral body-scanning?
faq.answer: Unilateral body-scanning is the body-scan method you learn in Step 4 (Stage 1.4). You move your attention through the body one side at a time, part by part, observing whatever sensations are present in each area with equanimity before moving on. It builds directly on the systematic body awareness from Step 3, training you to feel sensations more clearly and to stay accepting of them without reacting. It's the foundation for the more advanced scanning methods (symmetrical scanning, sweeping, and transversal scanning) introduced in the later stages.
faq.keywords: unilateral, body scanning, body scan, step 4, stage 1.4, part by part, technique

faq.question: What is loving-kindness (metta) meditation and how do I practise it?
faq.answer: Loving-kindness (metta) meditation is the main practice introduced in Step 9 (Stage 4.1), done twice daily during this stage. It cultivates warmth and goodwill — first toward yourself, then gradually extended outward to others — and research links it to greater present-centredness, less self-preoccupation, and reduced reactivity. In the app, listen to the guided loving-kindness track in the Meditations section of Stage 4.1. It's a natural progression from the earlier stages: having learned to meet your own sensations with equanimity and to stay balanced with others, you now actively develop compassion, which supports the self-compassion and ethical living that round out the program. If you're going through a period of resenting yourself or others, a shorter guided loving-kindness meditation is also recommended.
faq.keywords: loving-kindness, metta, compassion, meditation, stage 4.1, step 9, goodwill, how to

faq.question: Should I use the guided or the timer (silence) bipolar exposure track?
faq.answer: There are two bipolar exposure tracks in the Meditations section of Stage 2.1: a Guided version and a Silence/Timer-only version. Use the Guided track for your first few practices, until you are sufficiently familiar with the technique. Once you know the steps well, switch to practising in silence with the Bipolar Exposure Timer. This mirrors how the whole program works — you lean on the guided audio while learning a technique, then move to silent practice as you become more independent, which strengthens your own capacity to apply the skill without prompts.
faq.keywords: bipolar exposure, guided, timer, silence, track, audio, stage 2.1, which one, difference

faq.question: Why does equanimity matter so much during exposure?
faq.answer: In Stage 2, exposure isn't about white-knuckling through a feared situation — it's about facing it while staying equanimous toward the body sensations that arise. When you approach an avoided situation and observe the co-emerging sensations with acceptance rather than reacting to them, you break the cycle that kept the avoidance going: the situation stops automatically triggering distress-and-escape. Practising equanimity during exposure is what actually generalises the benefit, so that confidence built facing one situation carries over to others. This is also why the body-scanning skills come first — you need to be able to feel and accept sensations with equanimity before applying that skill in real, challenging situations.
faq.keywords: equanimity, exposure, avoidance, sensations, confidence, stage 2, generalise, reactivity

faq.question: What are the four steps of experiential ownership?
faq.answer: Experiential ownership is the Stage 3 skill for staying balanced in tense interpersonal situations, and it has four steps: (1) Take responsibility for your experience — when tension arises, do a very quick whole-body sweep (2-3 seconds) and bring equanimity to wherever sensations feel most intense, recognising that your reaction is happening in your body. (2) Recognise the other person's experience — acknowledge that they, too, are experiencing their own sensations and reactions. (3) Relinquish responsibility for the other's experience — accept that you are not responsible for what arises inside them. (4) Offer a helpful response — from that balanced place, choose a constructive reply rather than a reactive one. It builds on the sweeping-en-masse practice from this stage, which makes the rapid body scan in Step 1 possible, and it lays the groundwork for mindful assertiveness.
faq.keywords: experiential ownership, four steps, interpersonal, responsibility, equanimity, sweeping, response

faq.question: What is mindful assertiveness?
faq.answer: Mindful assertiveness is the Step 8 (Stage 3.2) skill for expressing yourself honestly and constructively while staying equanimous. It combines the experiential-ownership skill you developed the previous week with a structured way of communicating (the seven assertive statements), so you can address interpersonal tensions — stating your perspective, needs, or boundaries — without being driven by reactivity, aggression, or avoidance. Because you stay grounded in equanimity toward your own body sensations during the exchange, you're able to choose a proactive, respectful response rather than reacting habitually. It's the interpersonal application of everything practised in the earlier stages.
faq.keywords: mindful assertiveness, assertive, interpersonal, seven statements, equanimity, communication, step 8

faq.question: What are the seven assertive statements?
faq.answer: The seven assertive statements are the structured (but adaptable) way of communicating you learn in Step 8 (Stage 3.2), used once you have grounded yourself in experiential ownership and chosen a good time and setting. They are intended for when the other person is reasonably calm and open to dialogue (if they are very upset, use experiential ownership instead). The seven are: (1) State the facts — describe the situation or behaviour objectively, without judgment. (2) State how you feel — use "I" statements to own your feelings without blaming. (3) State what you think — share your perspective, again using "I" statements. (4) State your possible error in judgment — acknowledge you might have misread the situation, which lowers defensiveness. (5) State what you want — clearly express your need or point of view. (6) Thank the person if they cooperate — express genuine appreciation, reinforcing goodwill. (7) Negotiate — if your request isn't met, work together toward a win-win solution. Staying in experiential ownership throughout helps you remain composed as you move through them.
faq.keywords: seven assertive statements, seven statements, assertiveness, facts, feel, think, want, negotiate, step 8

faq.question: When should I move from the guided audio to practising in silence?
faq.answer: Most practices in the program come in two forms: a guided audio track and a silent (timer-only) version. The general approach is the same throughout: use the guided track while you are still learning a technique, and once you are sufficiently familiar with it, move to practising in silence using the timer. Practising without the audio sharpens your focus, helps you stay alert, and builds your ability to apply the skill on your own rather than depending on prompts. There is no fixed deadline — if a practice still feels unsettled, it is completely fine to take a few more days or a week or two on the guided version before switching. You will see this pattern across the program: for example, mindfulness of breath, body-scanning (with its "Withdrawing Instructions" and "Practicing in Silence" tracks), and bipolar exposure (Guided first, then the Timer) all follow the same guided-then-silent progression.
faq.keywords: guided, silence, silent, timer, audio, withdrawing instructions, when to switch, practice, independent

faq.question: What does practising with 'strong determination' mean?
faq.answer: Strong determination (adhitthana) is a practice attitude introduced around Step 4, drawn from the Vipassana tradition MiCBT is based on. It means making a firm commitment, for the duration of a sitting, to stay still, keep your attention on the body, and remain as equanimous as possible — sitting completely still while scanning, as a way of accepting whatever experience arises rather than shifting to escape discomfort. It's considered one of the most powerful skills for effective practice, because holding firm and welcoming whatever comes up — without getting tangled in it — is exactly what allows equanimity to grow, both in meditation and in daily life. In short, you learn to 'not mind so much.'
faq.keywords: strong determination, adhitthana, stillness, immobile, equanimity, attitude, sitting

faq.question: What is the MIET?
faq.answer: MIET stands for the Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Exposure Task. It's a brief, structured technique you learn in Step 4 (Stage 1.4) for using equanimity in real-life stressful or painful moments. It takes about 30 seconds: when distress arises, you locate the strongest sensation in your body and observe it through four characteristics — mass, motion, temperature, and density — rating it before and after observing it with equanimity. In short, it extends the body-scanning skills you've been practising into everyday situations, so you can feel, accept, and let go of difficult sensations as they happen rather than reacting habitually. In the app, you record your MIET practice using the MISS assessment (there is no separate MIET screen).
faq.keywords: miet, mindfulness-based interoceptive exposure task, technique, step 4, equanimity, sensations, distress, miss, record

faq.question: When do I learn the MIET, and where do I find it in the app?
faq.answer: The technique is introduced in Step 4 (Stage 1.4). To get started, listen to the short introduction track and the guided exercise in the Meditations section of Stage 1.4 in the app. The introduction gives you the essential background, and the guided track walks you through the technique step by step until you feel confident using it on your own. You'll keep using it from Step 4 onwards, and it's explored in more depth for interpersonal situations in Weeks 7 and 8.
faq.keywords: miet, when, introduced, step 4, stage 1.4, meditations, app, find

faq.question: What are the steps of the MIET?
faq.answer: The technique has six quick steps: (1) Identify the area of strongest sensation — scan your body during the distressing moment to find where the sensation feels strongest. (2) Rate how intense it is, from least to most intense. (3) Observe the four characteristics in that area — how warm or cool, tight or loose, still or moving, and light or heavy it feels (temperature, density, motion, mass). (4) Apply equanimity — with unconditional acceptance, observe the predominant characteristics for about 30 seconds (roughly seven to nine breaths), without judgment or reactivity. (5) Observe the four characteristics again — notice whether anything has changed. (6) Rate the intensity again, from least to most. Comparing before and after shows how the sensation shifted through equanimity. You record these before/after observations in the app using the MISS assessment.
faq.keywords: miet, steps, how to, mass, motion, temperature, density, equanimity, 30 seconds, miss

faq.question: How do I record my MIET practice in the app?
faq.answer: In the app, you record your MIET practice using the MISS (Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Signature Scale) — there is no separate MIET screen. The MISS captures exactly what the technique asks you to notice. After or during an intense experience, tap Assessments and select MISS, then: rate the qualities of the body sensations using the four sliding scales (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity) at their most intense — this is your pre-equanimity rating; apply equanimity to those sensations for about 30 seconds; then record your post-equanimity rating on the same four scales. Make sure all four dots are moved so the line is drawn, then save. The change between your before and after ratings is the record of your MIET practice, and the MISS graph tracks it over time.
faq.keywords: miet, record, miss, where, assessments, mass, temperature, motion, fluidity, before, after, app

faq.question: Is the MIET about suppressing emotions or stopping myself from acting?
faq.answer: No — it's the opposite. Because emotions are made up of body sensations, and the technique guides you to pay attention to them with unconditional acceptance, you learn to feel and accept emotions more fully rather than suppressing them. It's also not about preventing action. By training you to reduce automatic reactivity, it actually frees you to choose the most proactive, considered response in the moment, rather than being driven by habit.
faq.keywords: miet, suppress, emotion, prevent, action, reactivity, acceptance, misconception

faq.question: What is the co-emergence model of reinforcement (CMR)?
faq.answer: The co-emergence model of reinforcement (often abbreviated CMR) is the central framework MiCBT uses to explain how we process experience and how habits form. It describes four functions of the mind that depend on each other: (1) Sensory perception — becoming aware of a situation through the senses, including noticing thoughts and internal body states; (2) Evaluation — the mind judging the situation, consciously or subconsciously, as good, bad, or neutral; (3) Co-emerging body sensations — sensations that arise together with that evaluation; and (4) Reaction — the response we make. The key insight is that we don't react to situations directly; we react to the body sensations that co-emerge with our evaluation of them. Under stress this system falls into 'disequilibrium' — attention is pulled away from sensory perception and body sensations toward evaluation and reaction, so we rely more on stored assumptions and lose touch with early emotional signals in the body. Mindfulness training restores the balance, which is why developing equanimity toward body sensations is so central to the whole program.
faq.keywords: co-emergence, coemergence, model, reinforcement, four functions, evaluation, sensations, reaction, theory, CMR, c.m.r

faq.question: I’m not able to relax despite really trying to. I can only get about 20% of my body to relax. It’s frustrating, and I’m starting to dislike this exercise. Am I doing it wrong?
faq.answer: It might seem counterintuitive, but the purpose of PMR isn’t to achieve 100% relaxation. The real goal is to carefully notice the contrast between tension (when you tighten the muscles) and relaxation (when you release that tension). This week, you’re training your mind to recognize these differences, which will help you notice and release tension more effectively in daily life. Interestingly, the sessions where relaxation feels difficult are often the most valuable. So, when you notice that it’s hard to relax muscles after tightening them, approach this as a learning experience! Being without aversion toward tension and without craving for relaxation will help you be more accepting of the unavoidable tensions that arise in daily life, even those that you cannot readily release.
faq.keywords: relaxation, relax, counterintuitive, frustrating, carefully, starting, exercise

faq.question: I have bulging discs in my back, and I feel scared of tensing my back in case I injure it further.
faq.answer: It’s important to be gentle with any areas that require special care. When the instructions suggest arching your back, adapt the movement to suit your body’s needs. For example, you can substitute a gentle stretch or, if necessary, simply bring your awareness to the area and notice whatever sensations are already present, without adding any small movements. In general, even when the audio instructions are to tighten muscles even “tighter,” avoid overly tensing muscles. The appropriate amount is one that doesn’t hurt.
faq.keywords: back, instructions, substitute, important, movement, bulging, tensing

faq.question: I feel anxious when I do PMR. Am I doing something wrong, or is this just too difficult for me?
faq.answer: It’s common to feel unsettled during the first few PMR sessions. However, the practice of PMR does not create anxiety in and of itself. This anxiety can stem from worries about not performing the exercise correctly, from taking time away from other activities or responsibilities, or from bringing physical sensations of anxiety that were already there into greater awareness. As PMR itself does not train skills to address anxious thoughts, relaxing during practice can allow existing concerns and anxieties to become more prominent. During your practice, do your best to disengage from any anxious or distracting thoughts that may be pulling your attention away from the body. Then refocus attention on the physical sensations of tensing and relaxing each muscle group. If anxiety persists, try practicing with your eyes open for the first few sessions. Once you feel more comfortable, gradually shift to practicing with your eyes closed.
faq.keywords: anxiety, feel, performing, something, difficult, unsettled, sessions

faq.question: I have some flashbacks during PMR. I can handle them, but I wonder if I would be better off skipping this practice.
faq.answer: Experiencing flashbacks during PMR can happen for those with a history of trauma. In your previous email, you mentioned past trauma. Relaxation can sometimes bring painful memories into awareness, which may explain why individuals with Post Traumatic Stress Disorder often avoid relaxation by engaging in distracting activities like watching TV late into the night, using substances, or overworking. If you experience flashbacks or an initial increase in anxiety during PMR, there are a few ways to approach the practice. You can keep your eyes half open for the first few sessions to help reduce feelings of vulnerability and provide a sense of control. Over time, as your confidence builds, you may feel more comfortable closing your eyes. If closing your eyes constantly triggers flashbacks, it’s perfectly fine to keep them open and proceed with PMR at your own pace. You can also focus your attention on the sensations of your feet on the floor or the sensations of sitting on the chair supporting you. This grounding practice can help anchor you in the present moment and reduce the intensity of flashbacks. If you are experiencing severe PTSD symptoms, work with your therapist to ease into relaxation training gradually.
faq.keywords: flashbacks, trauma, experiencing, relaxation, mentioned, sometimes, awareness

faq.question: I’ve practiced other forms of relaxation before, and it was suggested that I lie down. Can I lie down with this one, too?
faq.answer: It’s better to practice PMR while seated for two main reasons. First, lying down can lead to drowsiness, and one of the key goals of PMR is to learn how to remain fully awake while feeling deeply relaxed so you can apply this skill in daily life. All upcoming practices, such as mindfulness of breath and body-scanning, will also be practiced while seated, as this helps maintain alertness. Training yourself to be alert and deeply relaxed while seated during PMR this week will set you up for more alert, effective meditation practices next week! Posture plays an important role in supporting this alert, wakeful mental state.
faq.keywords: relaxation, drowsiness, practiced, suggested, practice, reasons, feeling

faq.question: I’m struggling to make time for practice. Can I just practice once instead of twice daily?
faq.answer: While practicing once daily is better than not practicing at all, research shows that more frequent practice leads to better results [16]. You’ll likely experience greater benefits if you commit to practicing twice daily. If finding time feels challenging, consider reorganizing your activities so your self-c are doesn’t come last … again. We often prioritize others’ needs out of duty or compassion, but neglect our own needs. This program is an opportunity to improve self-c are, which will also support the development of self-c ompassion in Stage 4 of the program. Our brains have evolved to prioritize tasks based on what we expect the rewards to be. If you currently find it more rewarding to focus on others’ needs than your own, it’s natural to prioritize their care over your own. Behavioral psychology explains this as a conditioned response, where perceived gains or losses shape our actions. These conditioned tendencies can make it hard to prioritize self-care, including practicing twice daily. Changing this pattern involves recognizing that investing in your well-being benefits not only you but also those around you. Instead of overthinking, try to approach twice-daily practice as a nonnegotiable part of your day. Developing the habit of consistent self-care is one of the most valuable outcomes—arguably even more important than learning to relax!
faq.keywords: practice, practicing, better, daily, once, struggling, experience

faq.question: I’ve tried PMR a couple of times, but I don’t like it because I dislike the feeling of tightening my muscles.
faq.answer: This happens occasionally. In MiCBT, the PMR technique we use emphasizes the contrast between tension and relaxation. Highlighting this difference makes it easier to detect tension and release it more effectively during daily life. If this contrast is not sufficiently clear during practice, PMR’s benefits may not carry over as strongly into the rest of your day. Another reason tensing is helpful is that during periods of anxiety or high stress, we can feel a little disconnected from the body. At these times, tensing can help us feel grounded in the body. That said, if deliberately tensing muscles feels uncomfortable or isn’t possible, there’s an alternative method of practicing PMR in MiCBT that is also very helpful. Instead of a strong contraction, bring your attention to each muscle group following the same sequence and gently contract the muscles— the contraction might be just at the threshold of awareness—and then allow relaxation, to whatever degree is possible. This approach can be very beneficial for managing subtle tension and stress throughout the day. Proceeding with the Next Chapter The most effective way to approach this book is to read one chapter each week. Begin reading Week 2 when you are ready to start the practice of mindfulness of breath. Aligning your reading with your practice prevents unnecessary confusion that might arise from reading too far ahead and allows for a natural balance between intellectual understanding and experiential learning. By pacing yourself, you’ll build a strong foundation for the skills and insights that follow. Happy practicing!
faq.keywords: tension, occasionally, highlighting, tightening, emphasizes, relaxation, difference

faq.question: I get caught in memories of old girlfriends when we had a good time, and my imagination goes wild. I can’t help having strong desires for sex when I practice. It’s like I’m obsessed or something.
faq.answer: Ah … craving! What you’re experiencing is neither abnormal nor obsessive. Your practice is simply reflecting what is stored in your memory and how it is stored. When these memories arise, they often trigger corresponding sensations in the body. This can be a warm tingling in the abdomen or face, a sense of pressure, or temperature changes in various areas, or in any number of ways. Interestingly, this process can work both ways. In future practices, see whether certain sensations in the body are then followed by these memories emerging in the mind. Sometimes it’s the memory that generates the body sensation, while other times it’s the body sensation that triggers the memory. Table 2.2 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. O Response inhibition Response inhibition rate from 1 to 10 how rate from 1 to 10 how R frequently you were frequently you were able to notice dis- able to notice distractions and bring tractions and bring R attention back to the attention back to the Morning breath. 1 = once or Evening breath. 1 = once or Practice Duration twice; 10 = almost all practice Duration twice; 10 = almost all Day Date (circle) (minute) the time (circle) (minutes) the time Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/No Wednesday Yes/No Yes/No Thursday Yes/No Yes/No Friday Yes/No Yes/No Saturday Yes/No Yes/No Sunday Yes/No Yes/No It’s possible that you’ve recently experienced conscious or subconscious sexual cravings, and these sensations remained just beneath your conscious awareness until your practice allowed you to perceive them. And here’s something to explore further: could it be that it is actually the body sensations that you crave, rather than sex itself? Try to observe these phenomena as objectively as possible during your practice, approaching even the experience of craving itself with equanimity, so that you make good use of your cravings! That said, don’t spend too long on body sensations this week. We’ll shift to mindfulness of body sensations next week. For now, observe the experience of craving briefly, noticing how it is experienced in the body and the mind, and then let it be and return your attention to the breath as soon as possible. Abandoning attachment to craving through single-pointed attention on your breath is a skillful approach to practice this week.
faq.keywords: memories, practice, stored, experiencing, girlfriends, imagination, reflecting

faq.question: I’ve been getting a strong pain in my lower back during practice. I wonder if it’s related to an accident I had 15 years ago. I had thought it had gotten better, but it seems that this practice is bringing the pain back. I’ve tried to work on my posture, but it’s not going away, and now I’m finding I don’t want to practice. I’ve kept practicing twice a day, but I’m afraid it’s going to get worse!
faq.answer: It may sound strange to say “well done,” but it’s good to know that you practiced even with the discomfort. That takes courage and commitment! Now, about your concern: mindfulness practice doesn’t cause or bring on back pain. Rather, it helps you become aware of what’s already present in your body. The practice itself doesn’t create sensations; it brings to light sensations that may have been just under the surface of your awareness. In other words, it allows you to feel what is already there. For your peace of mind, you might consider checking with your doctor to rule out any medical issues. At this early stage of your practice, if the uncomfortable sensations are manageable, the key is to not give them too much significance. Continue practicing calmly, without attaching negative judgments to the sensations. Feel the discomfort with equanimity, and then systematically return your attention to the breath. By fully re-engaging attention with the breath, you can avoid getting caught in stories or judgments about the sensations. If the sensations are intense to the extent that you cannot focus on the breath, then bring your attention to the area of most intense sensation for 30 seconds to a minute. Observe the sensations with curiosity and openness, noticing their qualities without taking them personally. These sensations are just transient experiences arising in the body—they are not “your pain.” They are impermanent, with a beginning, a middle, and an end. Research suggests that focusing on the area of discomfort with equanimity can significantly reduce the intensity of unpleasantness within 30 seconds! [33, 34] We’ll explore this technique further in Week 4. As you practice, remind yourself that these sensations do not define you. They are not permanent and do not belong to you. Instead, they are simply arising in this moment. With time and patient observation, you may find your relationship with the discomfort shifting, even if the sensations themselves persist. Remember, everything changes, including these sensations.
faq.keywords: practice, going, pain, back, mindfulness, practicing, discomfort

faq.question: I don’t really believe mindfulness practice is for me. My friends told me so many good things about this practice, but I don’t think I can do it. And honestly, I feel guilty sitting down twice a day for 30 minutes when I have so many things to do.
faq.answer: Doubt is a common theme when we start this practice. It typically falls into one of three categories: doubt about the technique, doubt about the teacher, or doubt about oneself. It sounds like you may be dealing with self-doubt. Is this something you notice in other areas of your life? Our established beliefs often shape how we approach challenges, including mindfulness. If you tend to lack self-confidence, this can affect your belief in your ability to succeed with the practice. But here’s the good news: this could be an opportunity to work with and reduce self-doubt. To do this, you’ll need to be attentive to any thoughts of doubt and the body sensations that arise alongside them. Instead of reacting to these sensations, observe them with curiosity and kindness. Notice that thoughts of self-doubt often center on “I” and “me.” This focus on the self can amplify feelings of inadequacy, as we can always find something to criticize about ourselves. It can be a real relief to shift from judging yourself to focusing on the breath, remaining kind and patient with yourself. Abandon all expectations about yourself during the practice. When self-critical thoughts arise, see them for what they are—just mental phenomena, not truths about you. Let go of the view that these thoughts define you. You can make it a key focus of your mindfulness practice this week, as an experiment!
faq.keywords: doubt, practice, things, many, mindfulness, categories, typically

faq.question: Is being focused on something, like when I’m really absorbed in a task, the same as mindfulness?
faq.answer: It depends! While being mindful always involves attentiveness, not all attentiveness is mindfulness. For instance, a cat poised in front of a mouse hole or a sniper ready to shoot are both examples of being fully concentrated on the “here and now.” However, in these cases, the focus is driven by craving (catching the mouse) or aversion (fear of being attacked). This type of attention lacks the qualities of “complete” or “right” mindfulness. In mindfulness, attention is free from craving or aversion. For instance, when we focus on the breath during meditation, the aim is not to crave peace of mind or avoid unwanted thoughts, but rather to observe the experience as it is, without attachment. Being attentive can help us complete tasks or remain alert, but mindfulness goes beyond that. It requires a state of openness, acceptance, and non-reactivity—qualities that help us relate to our experiences without identifying with them.
faq.keywords: attentiveness, mindfulness, something, absorbed, involves, instance, examples

faq.question: I feel very tired, and I need to relax my back. I find it difficult to sit with my neck and back straight. Can I lie down during the practice?
faq.answer: On days when we feel tired and have low energy, lying down to practice can seem very enticing! However, especially when you’re already tired, lying down tends to increase drowsiness—even if you’re not consciously aware of it. Your practice space might even turn into a “snoring camp”! If you’re feeling too tired or drowsy to practice, take a short rest. Having a 5-to 10-minute nap, followed by walking around to wake up, can help. Then, sit down to practice with refreshed energy. It’s also helpful to reflect on the desire to lie down. It may stem from a craving for relaxation and relief or an aversion to unpleasant body sensations. Avoidance can fool you if you’re not vigilant. If you want the best and quickest results, we encourage you to practice in the way that mindfulness meditators have done for the past 2,500 years. Unless there’s a serious medical reason that prevents you from sitting, it’s best to practice in a seated position.
faq.keywords: tired, practice, lying, feel, back, especially, drowsiness

faq.question: My mind is all over the place at times. I still get caught up in thoughts for ages before I realize it is happening. How do you stay focused?
faq.answer: This is common, no need for alarm! That moment of recognizing that attention has been caught up in thinking is actually very valuable. Congratulate yourself for making the effort to notice that this has occurred, because this is an important skill in and of itself. The quickest way to train your mind is to have the clear intention and firm determination that you will return attention to your breath within 3 seconds of noticing a thought arising. This is called the “3-second rule.” You might have observed that it’s easier to recognize a thought the moment it appears than after a few seconds, once you’ve already become engaged with it. If you don’t let go of the thought immediately, you can easily get carried away and lost in it once you have succumbed to the temptation of thinking, as thinking is surprisingly addictive. Thinking is addictive because we get attached to feeling the pleasant or unpleasant body sensations that co-emerge with certain thoughts and to the brain chemicals that the brain produces at the same time. For example, a very pleasant thought will co-emerge with a very pleasurable body sensation and the associated dopamine, which can be very reinforcing. We also identify with thoughts and feel that they are part of who we are. You may already have noticed that it’s easier to let go of thoughts that don’t have an emotional charge. In contrast, emotionally charged thoughts often co-emerge with strong body sensations, capturing your attention and making them harder to release. When this happens, try identifying where these sensations are in the body. Shift your attention to that area and observe the sensations equanimously. Focus there until the intensity subsides, and then gently return your attention to the breath. Cultivating tolerance and patience is also essential for working with thoughts. Remind yourself that these are just thoughts and all of them share the same fundamental quality: they arise and pass away, and none of them are permanent. Not getting what we want–whether it’s focus or calmness– while remaining patient strengthens our ability to respond similarly in daily life. This happens because we use the same brain in both contexts. When we develop patience in meditation, it naturally carries over into everyday situations.
faq.keywords: caught, congratulate, recognizing, happening, attention, thoughts, thinking

faq.question: During the meditation, I often ruminate over past issues with my ex, and I worry about what’s going to happen in the future. I noticed that when I bring my attention to my breath, I immediately feel better. This week, I kept on doing this, but it’s like my mind is obsessive, and these thoughts keep coming back. Should I keep doing the same thing? It doesn’t seem to be working.
faq.answer: Redirecting attention from an unhelpful mental state to a neutral object of attention such as the breath can be helpful in some contexts. However, this is not likely to address the deeper aspects of the problem, which is why it doesn’t seem to be working. When you refocus on the breath, you may be avoiding the sensations that co-emerge with your rumination and worry. Until the intensity of these sensations diminishes through mindful and equanimous attention, the associated thoughts are likely to persist. Think of a thought as a helium balloon attached to a hook by a rope. The hook represents the body sensations that come with challenging thoughts. Until the rope is untied, the balloon cannot float freely. Similarly, unhelpful thoughts may continue to resurface until the underlying sensations are acknowledged and released. When intrusive thoughts are persistent, interrupt your focus on the breath and redirect your attention to the area of strongest sensation in your body. Observe the sensations with curiosity and acceptance, recognizing them as body sensations with the same fundamental characteristics as a thought: they are impermanent, therefore they cannot belong to you or be part of who you are, and identifying with them and reacting to them only brings more suffering. In daily life, too, refocusing attention on the breath is better than worrying, but it may also become a way of avoiding what’s happening in your body. Instead, notice how thoughts arise alongside body sensations and how these sensations influence the mind. By observing this interplay, you can begin to untie the “rope,” allowing both the thoughts and sensations to subside more naturally.
faq.keywords: attention, breath, doing, keep, immediately, redirecting, meditation

faq.question: I keep thinking of my divorce and the kids, and I find it very difficult to focus after just a few seconds. I really prefer the PMR practice because I could be more present and less distracted. I’m not sure what’s the best way to go about it, but I don’t think I’m ready to move on to mindfulness of breath.
faq.answer: Since PMR didn’t resolve the problem of thinking about difficult topics, continuing with it isn’t likely to help you stay more present after the practice ends. In contrast, mindfulness practice is like wearing a good pair of glasses—it helps you see clearly what is usually blurred. Through mindfulness, you begin to understand the natural tendencies of your mind. What you are beginning to notice during practice is that the mind is agitated and scattered. You’re beginning to see the process of thinking, rather than just the content of your thoughts. This is progress! Now, your task is to observe this mental state mindfully without resenting it for being the way it is. Simply acknowledge that this agitation is impermanent and will subside in time. Resisting or resenting it only prolongs the agitation. This habit of wishing things were different is what creates many of our challenges in life. We don’t want them to be as they are. Mindfulness gives you the opportunity to change this habit of resenting internal experience and wishing it was different. Accepting your internal experience as it is, in some ways, is accepting parts of yourself. This alone can calm the mind both in meditation and in daily life. It would be helpful for you to continue practicing mindfulness of breath for a week, but this time all on your own, without the recorded audio instructions. Since you are already familiar with the instructions, practicing in silence allows you to focus fully on the breath without the potential distraction or “lullaby” soothing effect of the recording, which might reduce vigilance. Practice with patience, tolerance for your experience, and discernment. This means keeping in mind that we are not seeking any particular result. We are simply observing the way things are—the reality of the present moment as it manifests itself when you breathe in and out, from moment to moment—accepting whatever we discover. You’ve mentioned concerns about the divorce and your children, which may be contributing to the restlessness. Children often find comfort in their parents’ emotional stability and calmness. This doesn’t mean being free of agitation but learning to remain steady even amidst challenging thoughts. During practice, commit to focusing on the breath and nothing else, keeping the mind stable despite the underlying agitation. As counterintuitive as it may seem, the more you allow the mind to stay with the breath, the sooner the agitation will begin to settle.
faq.keywords: practice, mindfulness, difficult, thinking, present, distracted, continuing

faq.question: When I practice, I have flashbacks of something bad that happened, and I become anxious. It put me off practicing after the first 2 days. Can I meditate with music in the background? I’ve done this in the past, and it helped distract me from the memories, and then it felt like I could practice better.
faq.answer: Flashbacks and anxiety during mindfulness practice are actually relatively common, as many of us have experienced painful situations in life. Remember that practicing mindfulness is not only about concentrating, but also about building awareness of how our mind and body interact and function. While background music may have been soothing, it did not address the underlying painful memories, which are resurfacing during your practice nowadays. Music served as a way to avoid discomfort, offering temporary relief but not resolving the emotional charge of the memories. This avoidance, while initially offering temporary relief, can actually intensify the emotional charge of these memories through “negative reinforcement.” Each time you reduce discomfort by distracting yourself, you strengthen the habit of distraction. One of the problems with avoidance is that we cannot always avoid the memory, and the distress it creates doesn’t get resolved. This is an opportunity to look more deeply at what these memories are made of. It’s not the thoughts or memories themselves that create the reaction; it is the accompanying body sensations that provoke discomfort and anxiety. By observing the sensations that co-emerge with the memories, you can deepen your understanding of the physicality of your emotional experience and further recognize the habitual tendency to react with aversion in order to avoid discomfort. Our minds are conditioned to react emotionally when sensations in the body become intense. This process is something you can directly observe, dissect, and understand in greater and greater detail in the laboratory of your practice. This mindful observation allows you to be sufficiently detached from the emotional intensity of the memory and view it as just a mental event, rather than as part of who you are—you learn to not get caught up in it, to not make it yours or you. If the sensations become particularly intense, consider shifting your focus from the breath to the body sensations themselves. Direct your attention to the area of strongest sensation and study it carefully. Is there a predominance of certain characteristics, like temperature, heaviness, movement, or solidity? Is it dense or diffuse? Is it hot, warm, or cold? Is it heavy or light? Stay with it for a little while, remaining as calm and patient as possible. Keep in mind that the actual event is in the past and cannot harm you in the present. What matters now is how you relate to the sensations accompanying the thoughts in the present. Note to the reader: While the approach described above can be very helpful, working with traumatic memories requires careful attention to your capacity and safety. If you find yourself consistently overwhelmed, consider practicing under the guidance of a qualified therapist, preferably one trained in integrating mindfulness with exposure techniques. Their expertise can provide you with the support and tools necessary to work through these experiences safely and effectively.
faq.keywords: practice, mindfulness, flashbacks, practicing, experienced, background, relatively

faq.question: Why is it important to move my attention during body-scanning in this particular order?
faq.answer: There are three reasons for this. The first is that scanning in the same order each time ensures you don’t forget any areas of the body, especially as the mind can be so easily distracted. This is not a big issue this week because you are using the audio instructions, but it can become an issue next week, when you’ll be practicing in silence. The second reason for scanning in this order is that our attention naturally gravitates toward areas with obvious sensations, making it easy to neglect parts of the body that are difficult to feel. This can happen without us being consciously aware of it! If we neglect these subtle sensations, we miss out on opportunities to feel the very early signs of change in the body, such as the early signs of emotions. So, by scanning your body in the same order each time, you give equal attention to all areas, ensuring you don’t miss any, including those that don’t have many sensations or that feel blank. This practice strengthens neural connections in the insula and somatosensory cortex, which will help you feel more of your body over time. The third reason for scanning in this particular order is to lay the groundwork for the more advanced methods that we will soon learn. Unilateral scanning is the most basic body-scanning method and one that we will only use for 2 weeks before starting more advanced scanning techniques. In future weeks, you’ll be learning techniques like “sweeping,” where you move your attention fluidly through the body in a single, continuous motion, eventually at the speed of your breath. This requires a vertical approach to scanning. While it’s fine to move horizontally in small areas, such as the face, prioritizing vertical scanning now will make it easier to progress later.
faq.keywords: order, body-scanning, particular, especially, distracted, important, attention

faq.question: How fast should I go to scan the whole body?
faq.answer: This week, the audio instructions will guide you through one full body cycle during your 30-minute session. A body cycle means scanning from the top of your head to the tips of your toes, and then back up again. The slower pace this week helps you become familiar with the practice and builds a foundation for mindfulness of body sensations. Next week, you’ll begin scanning at your own pace, completing at least two body cycles in each 30-minute session.
faq.keywords: body, cycle, instructions, scanning, through, session, minute

faq.question: I can’t feel body sensations. What am I looking for?
faq.answer: It’s entirely normal, especially when starting body-scanning, to experience some parts of the body as “blank,” with no noticeable sensations. As mentioned earlier in the chapter, this will gradually change over time as the brain “wires” to feel sensations in these areas. At times, though, it can seem like you can’t feel any sensations. This can happen when we expect body sensations to be “special” or distinct in some way. They are not! During body-scanning, we are simply observing ordinary, everyday sensations as they arise. Anything you feel counts as a body sensation. This might be sensations of temperature, such as feeling warmth or coolness in some part of your body. It could be sensations associated with movement, such as the movement of your chest wall or abdomen when breathing. It could be sensations of heaviness or lightness, such as feeling heaviness in the neck and other places due to fatigue. Sensations could also be related to the experience of density, such as feeling a sense of expansion or looseness when we are relaxed or joyful. We practice to observe our experiences in the body just as they are. If you can’t feel a particular area of your body, simply accept that you can’t feel sensations in this particular part at this moment. Keep attention on that area for up to 10 seconds. Bring an equal interest to each sensation, whether pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral, without taking it personally. As your ability to sustain attention and not get lost in thoughts improves week to week, you’ll be able to stay focused on blank spots more easily for up to 30 seconds before moving on. Remember: sensation or not, keep smiling and move on. This attitude of patience and acceptance cultivated during meditation carries over into daily life. When you don’t get what you want or encounter challenges, you’ll find yourself more tolerant, less agitated, and better equipped to respond skillfully. After all, the brain you use for meditation is the brain you also use in daily life. How you shape your brain during practice directly influences how it responds to everyday situations.
faq.keywords: sensations, body, body-scanning, especially, experience, noticeable, mentioned

faq.question: I’ve had strong feelings of sadness, frustration, and anxiety during my last few practices. Scanning through them in the body helped to resolve them a little, but they creep back. There was a recent incident involving my eight-year-old son that upset me, and even though the issue is now resolved, I still feel overly emotional about it.
faq.answer: It sounds like you are genuinely trying to remain objective and equanimous. This is a great opportunity to further develop your equanimity. First, it’s important to acknowledge that your emotional reaction is entirely normal—most people would react strongly if something were to happen to their child! Recognizing this can help you accept your experience with greater self-compassion. At the same time, your lingering distress suggests that while the external situation is resolved, thinking about it is still creating distress internally. Unresolved emotions consist of related thoughts or memories and their co-emerging body sensations. In your case, the sensations are co-emerging with memories of the incident involving your son, not the incident itself. Keeping this distinction in mind can help you bring genuine equanimity to the sensations. Your growing ability to feel body sensations means that you’re becoming more aware of subtle sensations in daily life that you might not usually notice. This is similar to using a magnifying glass—with enough magnification, even the pores on your skin can look like large holes! The more you refine your awareness of subtle sensations, the more those that were only mildly uncomfortable seem more intense. You need to ensure that you create a good balance between interoceptive awareness (which serves as your magnifying glass) and equanimity—i.e., a balance between feeling and accepting body sensations. This will not only soothe your emotional experience, but it will also increase your ability to feel even subtler sensations during practice. If you’re finding it challenging to sustain equanimity, try pausing your body-scanning practice briefly. Instead, focus on the area where sensations feel most intense. Rather than resisting or wishing the sensations away, examine the area of intensity carefully. Ask yourself: Do the sensations feel heavy or light? Do they move or are they still? What temperature do they have, if any? Are they dense or more diffuse? Keep attention on the area for 30–60 seconds and notice its changing nature, while accepting it fully. Doing this is likely to assist you in your effort to manage your sadness.
faq.keywords: emotional, eight-year-old, frustration, opportunity, acknowledge, equanimous, equanimity

faq.question: The scanning is going well, but I still can’t feel some parts of my chest and stomach, and I get frustrated. Am I doing something wrong?
faq.answer: Not at all. It’s perfectly normal not to feel sensations in every part of the body at this stage. There are two main reasons for this. First, you may have noticed that it’s much easier to feel sensations in the face, hands, and feet than in the torso. This is because there are more brain cells dedicated to feeling the face, hands, and feet than there are for the chest and abdomen. Second, it’s natural to dislike unpleasant sensations in the abdomen or chest, such as those that occur with anxiety or chronic stress. Over time, avoiding these sensations can “turn down the volume” of all sensations in those areas, making them harder to detect [74]. For this reason, it can take a little longer to feel sensations in the front part of the torso compared to other parts of the body. Remember, not feeling sensations in some parts of the body is also impermanent; it will change with consistent practice. When you encounter a “blank” area, try to maintain your attention calmly and equanimously on the blank area, as learning to be patient when we can’t feel sensations is also part of the practice. However, if you practice twice daily and find that larger areas remain blank for several days, then focus within those blank spots by expanding your attention to cover a wider area at a time, about 3–4 in. in diameter, for around 30 seconds. Increasing the size of the area you are paying attention to will increase the chances of feeling a sensation. Move on when you feel something or after approximately six to eight breaths.
faq.keywords: feel, frustrated, sensations, something, perfectly, scanning, stomach

faq.question: I feel agitated when I practice. Is that normal?
faq.answer: Yes, experiencing agitation or restlessness is common when we first start the practice. It’s nothing to be concerned about. In fact, this may be a valuable opportunity to carefully observe where sensations associated with agitation are experienced. Hold your attention there while remaining calm and equanimous for a minute or two. Investigate, dissect, and study what you witness with curiosity and genuine interest. Are they warm or cool? Still or moving? These sensations are impermanent, and just like everything else in life, they are not here to stay. It’s also important to remind yourself that the experience of agitation is just that: an experience. It doesn’t define you, and it’s not something you need to call “me” or “mine.” Remember this each time you start to become agitated about the agitation! If you observe the experience in an equanimous way without identifying with it, it won’t take long before the agitation transforms into something else. If the agitation is caused by persistent thoughts, instead of focusing on the thoughts or their co-emerging sensations, focus on the breath and nothing else, keeping your mind as stable as possible despite the underlying agitation. Once your mind has settled, return to scanning the body. You might find it helpful to remember the acronym FAME: “Feel, Accept, and Move with Equanimity,” which was created by one of our clients. This can serve as a simple reminder of how to approach sensations and the attitude to bring to them during your practice.
faq.keywords: practice, experiencing, restlessness, opportunity, agitation, concerned, carefully

faq.question: I’ve noticed that my eyes move to the area of the body I’m focusing on. For example, when I try to feel the top of my head, my eyes look up. When I scan my left shoulder, my eyes look toward that shoulder. Basically, I scan with my eyes, too! It’s very tiring, and I get a small headache. Is it ok for my eyes to be moving like this?
faq.answer: This is a common experience when starting body- scanning. Many of us instinctively use our visual modality to “support” the movement of attention by visualizing the body parts we’re focusing on. This can lead to unintentional eye movements, as the brain is more accustomed to relying on vision than on interoception (the ability to sense internal body sensations). When we can’t easily feel certain areas of the body, the brain tries to “see” them instead. Doing this for 30 minutes can easily lead to a headache. Bruno recalls experiencing the same thing when he began practicing over 35 years ago. The sooner you stop tracking your sensations with your eyes, the more comfortable and effective your practice will become. As your ability to feel sensations develops, the habit of trying to also “see” them will naturally decrease. For now, simply notice when your eyes begin to move and gently rest them in one position. Focus on scanning the body without engaging your eyes. If this feels challenging, try opening your eyes slightly and resting your gaze on a fixed point on the floor a short distance in front of you while continuing to scan the body.
faq.keywords: eyes, focusing, shoulder, body, look, scan, instinctively

faq.question: When I do my mindfulness practice, I get very agitated and remorseful. I can’t settle my body, and my mind is too distracted to stay focused on sensations, so I didn’t practice as much as I was supposed to. I keep thinking about all the years I wasted staying in my marriage with a difficult man, and what it’s done to my children. He was harsh with them, too, and they’ve never been happy kids. I should have left 10 years earlier, but I was scared about not being able to cope on my own.
faq.answer: Your feelings of remorse are understandable, and this experience is common when difficult emotions from the past resurface. It’s important to recognize that these feelings are part of the process and may even offer insight into what truly matters to you. However, the key is to avoid falling into self-blame. Using the practice to dwell on blaming yourself only agitates the mind further and reduces your ability to accept the past as it was. Last week, during mindfulness of breath practice, you began learning to notice thoughts as they pop up in your mind without perceiving them as parts of who you are, without identifying with them. This marked a shift from seeing your thoughts as part of “you” to recognizing them as transient mental events. The same principle applies to body-scanning. At this stage, you may still find it challenging not to identify with the sensations that arise, as they may feel deeply personal, like parts of yourself. However, as you continue practicing, you will develop the ability to observe these “bits of the self” arise and pass without clinging to them, whether they feel acceptable in the moment or not. This will help you notice these thoughts and feel the accompanying sensations while interrupting rumination and the suffering it creates. In turn, this will calm your mind. It’s also helpful to recognize that strong sensations of guilt can obscure other important aspects of your experience. For example, your deep concern for your children shows how much compassion you have for them. You stayed in a difficult marriage for years because you were afraid of not being able to provide for them on your own. Take a moment to reflect: Could it be that they have always felt the depth of your love for them? How does it feel to remind yourself of these aspects rather than focus on remorse and self-blame? If focusing on these positive aspects feels a bit too challenging, it may be because the co-emerging sensations are too intense to approach with equanimity. In this case, shift your attention to the breath and practice mindfulness of breath for a short while, or for the entire session, if helpful. Remember, your efforts to reduce anxiety not only benefit you but also positively impact your children, who will notice and feel the effects of your progress. While the past cannot be changed, each moment of practice is a step toward shaping a better future, for yourself and those you care about.
faq.keywords: difficult, practice, feelings, years, understandable, mindfulness, remorseful

faq.question: It’s very strange, when I started this practice last week, I began to feel some nausea during body-scanning. Not too much, but enough to feel uncomfortable. Is this normal? Is it possible that mindfulness could be harmful for some people?
faq.answer: No, being mindful is not harmful to people, but nausea does happen sometimes, and it passes. It can also happen during progressive muscle relaxation and mindfulness of breath, often as we become more aware of our emotions or worry about them. Remember, nausea doesn’t last forever, and, like other forms of discomfort, it is a good tool for developing equanimity. In fact, you couldn’t develop equanimity without discomfort. Just as we can only learn patience when we are impatient, we can only learn equanimity when we are reactive. It’s also useful to remember that nausea can arise in the context of a variety of conditions, both mental and physical, including anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, eating disorders, migraines, infection, food poisoning, or medication side effects, which sometimes occur when medication is taken on an empty stomach. Even dietary changes, such as eating too quickly or consuming spicy or fatty foods, can trigger nausea. If nausea persists outside of meditation and doesn’t seem directly related to it, it may be a good idea to consult your doctor to rule out any underlying health issues. It’s important to understand that the practice of sitting calmly and scanning the body does not inherently cause nausea. Instead, it may increase your awareness of subtle sensations linked to residual anxiety, which can manifest as nausea. Alternatively, a past memory of nausea might be retriggered during practice. When nausea arises, do your best to remain equanimous, reminding yourself that the discomfort is temporary and not dangerous in and of itself. If the sensations are too overwhelming during meditation, even after focusing equanimously on the four characteristics— mass, motion, temperature, and density—in the area where it is most intense for about half a minute, then focus calmly on the breath, continuing with mindfulness of breath for the rest of that session. If you find that the nausea increases or other intense experiences arise in the following few days, stop practicing body scanning and replace it with PMR for a few sessions. If you find that nausea has subsided and there is no need for a medical checkup, resume body scanning.
faq.keywords: mindfulness, harmful, nausea, people, happen, feel, body-scanning

faq.question: I’ve been having headaches during the body-scanning, usually after about 15–20 minutes every time I practice. I didn’t experience this during PMR or Mindfulness of Breath. I would appreciate your thoughts on what might be causing them.
faq.answer: There could be several factors contributing to the headaches. It’s common for people to put a lot of effort into trying to feel sensations, which can lead to tension headaches. If you find yourself in this situation, take a step back, abandon the strong desire to feel something specific, and simply notice what sensations are arising naturally, if any. It’s also worth considering whether the headaches come more frequently when you are scanning certain parts of the body. At times, some body sensations during our practice can feel similar to those we feel during stressful situations, such as past or present conflicts, painful memories, relationship or work dissatisfaction, and other issues we may not feel prepared to face at this moment, perhaps because of fears that they will be “opening a can of worms,” so to speak. The emotion we may not yet be ready to feel can still be subconscious, and we therefore can’t yet make sense of it. It’s natural to automatically, often subconsciously, attempt to suppress these emotions, as they often feel uncomfortable. Has there been any old issue intensifying or any new issue arising in your life? Avoiding these sensations during meditation can create much tension, which can result in headaches. Because this process often occurs at a largely subconscious level, do your best to remind yourself that the headache is simply an unpleasant sensation that will pass in time. Resisting or wishing it away can inadvertently increase its intensity, as tension and worry tend to amplify discomfort. The more relaxed and equanimous you remain during practice, the more likely the headache will dissipate naturally and with greater ease.
faq.keywords: headaches, body-scanning, contributing, mindfulness, experience, appreciate, sensations

faq.question: I cannot feel ordinary sensations clearly unless I visualize each area of my body. Is there something wrong with my brain?
faq.answer: There is nothing wrong with your brain. Where you can’t feel a sensation, simply acknowledge this without judgment—this acceptance helps maintain a calm and focused mind. Stay with each “blank spot” for up to 30 seconds, and then move on to the next part of your body, whether or not a sensation becomes noticeable. This approach reduces the tendency to visualize body parts. If, after three days, you still feel that there is little to no progress, consider switching to Mindfulness of Breath for the remainder of the week. This will help strengthen your ability to regulate attention, making it easier to sustain focus on areas with subtle or faint sensations when you return to bodyscanning the following week. Finally, let go of any judgments about your ability or performance. Rather than expecting to feel sensations because the audio instructions suggest it, simply notice whether you can feel sensations in a part of the body or not. Your worth is not defined by your ability to perceive body sensations. Skillful practice is characterized by making an effort to feel sensations while approaching your experience without reacting to it or identifying with it— in other words, practicing with equanimity.
faq.keywords: wrong, brain, feel, acknowledge, sensations, acceptance, visualize

faq.question: For the past few days, during meditation, I’ve been obsessing about roast pork. I can hear the pork ribs crackling in the oven, and I can even smell the exquisite aroma in my mind. And I just can’t help it, after my meditation, I have to cook something.
faq.answer: Cravings, especially for familiar things like food, are common during body-scanning and can be easily explained. Remember how we discussed in Week 2 that spontaneous thoughts are likely informed by our recent and frequent thoughts, or previous emotionally charged experiences? To revisit these ideas, the frequency effect refers to habitual thoughts that arise because of their prominence in daily life. For instance, if you often think about food, these thoughts are more likely to arise during meditation, especially during moments when your concentration dips. The co-emergence effect, on the other hand, happens when body sensations during meditation resemble sensations linked to past experiences. For example, if you consciously or subconsciously feel a pleasant body sensation similar to one you’ve felt when you were craving roast pork in the past, it acts as a memory cue, triggering the memory of craving roast pork. But this is only a memory of craving. It’s not craving in and of itself—it only becomes craving if you react to the memory by engaging with it, effectively making the past your present. During body-scanning, rather than focusing on the sensory memories themselves—such as the image of roast pork or hearing it crackle—redirect your attention to the pleasurable sensations that co-emerge with the memories. For example, when a pleasant memory of the smell of roast pork arises, shift your attention to the co-emerging pleasant body sensations and feel them equanimously, rather than focusing on the smell. Continue to feel the pleasant sensations, but without attachment to them or craving more. Just observe them with genuine curiosity for a little while, like a scientist observing the mechanisms of an experiment and collecting data. Which of the four characteristics is predominant: mass, motion, temperature, or density? Or is it perhaps a combination of these? See how impermanent they are when you remain aware and equanimous. The trap of being attached to sensations that are changing all the time will become clear—because of past attachment to them, we crave them when they are not present, and we remain unfulfilled. Through your practice of awareness and equanimity during meditation, you can learn to neutralize your cravings, which in turn will assist you in addressing craving-related behaviors in your daily life, like emotional eating, with greater freedom and understanding.
faq.keywords: meditation, pork, body-scanning, spontaneous, especially, obsessing, crackling

faq.question: I still can’t feel many sensations in my face when I scan, so I have been imagining the shape of my nose, cheeks, and ears to try to feel more. Is it okay to do that?
faq.answer: It’s very common to need more time to feel the whole body. When we can’t feel certain areas, the brain often automatically compensates by visualizing those parts—this occurs spontaneously. The brain is remarkable in its ability to problem-solve. When one function isn’t fully developed, like sensing certain body parts, another function, such as visualization, will come to the rescue. When you scan the body, you activate nerve cells contained in the somatosensory cortex, a strip of the brain responsible for processing sensations. Another region, the insula, becomes involved when scanning sensations linked to emotions. If the neural connections between some groups of cells in the somatosensory cortex and insula aren’t yet well-developed, there may be parts of the body that are still difficult to feel. For example, if the neurons that allow you to feel your ears aren’t sufficiently connected with each other, it might be hard to feel your ears. When this happens, the visual cortex will activate to help you imagine their shape. However, relying on visualization strengthens the neural pathways for seeing rather than feeling the body. If our goal was simply to become more attentive and concentrated, more focused in the present moment, then visualizing parts of the body and using verbal cues, like mantras, would certainly be helpful and welcome during our practice. But in MiCBT, our purpose is very different. We have a very strong rationale for training ourselves to feel the body. We train to feel body sensations to cultivate equanimity, not just concentration. In this way, we learn to not react with more craving and aversion toward sensations associated with emotions. Through this, we regulate both attention and emotions. If this resonates with you, do your best to just notice the blank spots when you scan the body and prevent craving or aversion from arising. Pay attention there for 30 seconds to a minute, or until you feel a sensation. When you move, do so “smilingly,” so to speak, without impatience, disappointment, or frustration. This is part of normal practice and is not a reason to feel defeated or incompetent. wee k 4: applying mind fulness skills in daily lif e 165 For now, accept that this is how it is at this moment, and that this, too, is impermanent. Over time, you’ll likely be able to feel every part of your body, except areas like hair or the outer nails. In the bigger picture, not feeling sensations when you scan the body is an opportunity to work on your patience and equanimity, so that you’ll be able to do the same in daily life when things don’t go as planned.
faq.keywords: feel, brain, automatically, spontaneously, compensates, visualizing, sensations

faq.question: I have serious pain in my knee when I practice, even though I’m sitting on a chair, not even cross-legged on the floor! I injured my knee at the gym about 5 years ago. Do you think the body-scanning is making it worse? What should I do about it?
faq.answer: Body-scanning doesn’t create pain; it only reveals what is already there. Think of it like washing dirty clothes—the dirt comes out, but it was in the clothes, not caused by the soap. Similarly, body-scanning is like soap; it allows you to notice existing sensations, but it doesn’t cause them. The intense sensations you’re feeling during practice were already there, or came up during practice, but not because of the practice. It’s common to feel the physical memory of old injuries when you start body-scanning, and this tends to decrease quickly over time. However, if you’re concerned about an underlying physical issue, it’s always a good idea to consult your doctor. In the meantime, remember our discussion regarding the four basic characteristics of body sensations. Since what we call “pain” is simply composed of intense sensations, it can be addressed with equanimity. Focus your attention on the most intense spot in your knee, staying with it as if you’re in the calm eye of a storm, patiently and equanimously, for up to 30 seconds. Which of the four characteristics is most predominant? Perhaps constriction? Or heat? Observe what happens when you genuinely allow and welcome the experience, rather than resisting or resenting it. If you find it difficult to tolerate the sensation, it may be because you’re identifying too closely with it, believing that this is “your” sensation and therefore “your” body, and, consequently, “your” pain. Even if you’re very committed to practicing in absolute immobility, gritting your teeth and sweating it out while resenting or hating the pain will only increase fear and aversion, and your mental state will be anything but equanimous. It’s like trying to break a stone with an egg; it simply doesn’t work! Remember that equanimity needs to be applied to all reactive habits, including unhelpful thoughts. Recognizing that “this is just a thought” or “this is just a sensation” helps minimize your identification with the experience, making it easier to remain equanimous even when it is very intense. Ensure that you relax into the four basic characteristics (mass, motion, temperature, and density) of the intense sensation and let those characteristics just be. Do your best to feel and accept them unconditionally. If the pain becomes too intense, then have a rest by moving your leg a little or have a small break from the practice and return to it a little later. By using intense sensations like pain to develop equanimity, you put the difficult experience to good use. It becomes an opportunity to develop patience and determination, and can lead to rapid changes in daily life. You turn potential suffering into “happiness capital,” and even pain can become acceptable when you know it’s not wasted and may even benefit you in the future.
faq.keywords: body-scanning, clothes, think, pain, knee, cross-legged, similarly

faq.question: I have problems falling asleep since I started scanning the body. Can I use my practice of mindfulness of breath to fall asleep? I also have strange dreams. Do you think they are related to my practice?
faq.answer: Let’s talk about your dreams first. Do you recall what we discussed about co-emergence? It describes how body sensations and thoughts are interconnected, often triggering one another. This connection makes it easier to understand how your dreams arise, and how it is that they often reflect the emotional tone of what you felt during the day. For example, if you’ve been worrying in the back of your mind about getting an injection tomorrow at a medical appointment required for traveling, your body-scanning practice may bring awareness to the body sensations associated with that anxiety. Once you’re asleep, if these sensations are still present, they can trigger anything stored in memory that involves similar types of sensations, influencing the content of your dreams. The dream itself can take the form of anything stored in your memory— any scenario, event, or person that you read about, heard about, or watched on the news or in a movie, at any age—that has become associated with similar sensations, even if seemingly unrelated to your current anxiety. In the example of being afraid to get an injection, the dream might involve someone wanting to poison you or chasing you down the street with a sword. This reflects how the mind connects bodily sensations with stored memories that involved similar sensations, weaving them into the fabric of your dreams. It’s important to note, however, that this connection isn’t always straightforward. Sometimes, we might encounter very strong experiences in daily life and yet not remember any dream at all, or we might dream about something completely unrelated. In general, you can observe a link between what you’ve been feeling recently and the emotional tone of the dream. Within the context of your practice, sudden changes in dreaming are often signs of memory stimulation or increased awareness of your current emotions, offering insight into co-emergence, even during sleep! You also say that it’s not been as easy to fall asleep this week. Most people report feeling more relaxed in daily life and having more energy by this stage of the program. This may be contributing to not needing as much sleep this week. There is also the possibility that your increased awareness of sensations when you are relaxing in bed before falling asleep is accompanied by more wee k 4: applying mind fulness skills in daily lif e 167 co-emerging thoughts. One thing that often helps to calm your mind and ease into sleep is to use your subtlest possible breath to reduce mental and emotional activity. If you want to give this a go, this is how: (1) Turn your alarm clock away so you’re not tempted to check the time. This reduces unnecessary worry. (2) Then, keeping your eyes closed, focus all your attention on the small spot below the nostrils above the upper lip, a spot no bigger than the size of a fingertip—about 1 cm2. (3) Gradually reduce the volume and depth of your breathing so that you can barely feel the faint touch of your breath on that small spot. Your breath should be so subtle that any less and you wouldn’t notice it at all. This results in decreased heart rate and the probability of agitation. When you reach that threshold of awareness, your breathing naturally becomes very shallow, and may occasionally pause on its own. Because you are still focusing on the task and your focus is so narrow, the inhibition pathways in the brain stay engaged, preventing the typical mind-wandering that usually kicks in when your mind is not busy and can keep you awake.
faq.keywords: practice, asleep, dreams, body, interconnected, co-emergence, mindfulness

faq.question: I’m still not 100% clear about why I have to scan the body. I read that mindfulness is just being in the present moment, so we can live our lives more fully. Why can’t I just focus on my breath, like I was doing a week ago? I really liked doing that. It really calmed my mind.
faq.answer: It’s wonderful that mindfulness of breath has helped calm your mind. This technique is a reliable way to rest your mind when needed and can always serve as a supportive practice. However, mindfulness of breath is just one part of the larger picture in this program. Body scanning adds another dimension to your practice by training interoceptive awareness—your ability to feel and observe sensations throughout your body. For this program to offer you maximum benefit, it’s essential to recognize that scanning the body is also scanning through our past, the storehouse of our important memories. Many memories have decayed to the extent that we can only experience them through their corresponding body sensations, without even knowing that what we are currently feeling in the body is the remains of a memory. Most sensations we feel are conditioned by past experiences. By observing these sensations equanimously during scanning, you prevent conditioned reactions, effectively freeing yourself from habitual responses tied to the past. Repeated across the whole body, over time, this process can also help you release unhelpful views and values that potentially limit your well-being and growth. Another critical reason for practicing body scanning is that it sharpens your ability to discern between wholesome and unwholesome thoughts. Recognizing helpful from unhelpful thoughts without feeling their immediate consequence in the body would require so much mental analysis that it would be unrealistic to attempt this with every thought that enters the mind. The most practical, and accurate, way of discerning whether a particular thought is helpful or unhelpful is to feel its immediate bodily consequences, becoming sensitive to any sensations that co-emerge.
