This practice allows us to more readily discern our motivations and intentions, empowering us to change them when they are likely to contribute to suffering. We can use this clarity to develop wisdom, thoughtfully assessing what is important to do or say in certain contexts and recognizing when it is better to refrain. With this discernment, we can choose to be more patient or refrain from performing verbal or physical actions that could be harmful to ourselves or to others. Mindfulness is a prerequisite for wisdom, and alongside wisdom arises compassion. When mindfulness is cultivated as the “right mindfulness,” as described by the Buddha, and not simply attentiveness devoid of discernment, it reveals the universality of suffering. Rather than seeing suffering as something unique to ourselves or others, we come to understand it as a shared human condition. Consequently, it helps you accept your own and other people’s suffering, even when it manifests as unawareness or reactive emotions. Coming back to your original question, if you were to only practice mindfulness of breath, your ability to detect subtle sensations in the body would not be as well developed. This means you might only notice the emergence of intense sensations once they have become too overwhelming to prevent reactivity. However, developing the ability to detect, understand, and let go of these subtler sensations is crucial, because some of them represent the early manifestations of emotions. By detecting and observing these subtler sensations with equanimity before they intensify, you can prevent emotional reactivity from arising in the first place. This is also why we’ll start to practice more advanced scanning techniques in the coming weeks, including methods like “sweeping” attention throughout the entire body rapidly. These practices will further enhance your ability to perceive increasingly subtle sensations and recognize early cues of distress.
faq.keywords: mindfulness, mind, breath, really, doing, supportive, wonderful

faq.question: When you talk about not identifying with sensations or thoughts, I kind of understand what you mean, but I’m still confused about who or what I am then. If there’s no “me” or “I,” then who is speaking to you right now? Isn’t that me? If I am not my “ego” or my “I,” then what am I? This is a bit confusing.
faq.answer: You are right, this can be confusing. The idea of egolessness, or not having a fixed sense of self, is often best explored through your own direct experience in meditation practice. However, some explanations can be helpful to clarify what we mean by “ego” and why it’s important to examine it. The “ego” refers to the part of the mind that creates a sense of personal identity. It’s not something you need to “get rid of,” nor is it something you can pinpoint—although we have a good idea of which brain areas are involved. The “I” is a sense of self that we construct moment by moment by identifying with what we perceive, think, feel, and do (you may recognize wee k 4: applying mind fulness skills in daily lif e 169 these as the four components of the co-emergence model introduced in Week 3: sensory perception, evaluation, body sensation, and reaction). This means that we are constructing and modifying our sense of self all the time, by identifying with our experiences of the body, such as its appearance, or through attachment to certain tastes, smells, sights, sounds, and tactile sensations. We identify with the thoughts that arise in the mind, including our beliefs and imagined futures, as well as the memories of events that shaped our lives. We identify with emotions through the sensations we feel in the body. We also identify with our actions, including routine behaviors and our ways of reacting. Over time, this ongoing identification builds a story that becomes the narrative of “me” or “I.” We also extend this sense of self to include the people, possessions, and roles we value. When we think of something as “mine,” we also perceive it as being a part of “me,” including “my family,” my culture,” “my best friends,” “my career,” “my football team,” “my political party,” etc. This can lead to strong attachments, as we naturally want to protect and preserve what feels like part of our identity. Problems arise when this constructed sense of self is challenged—whether by loss, change, or conflict. Because nothing is permanent, disruptions to this sense of self are inevitable—like when we lose a possession that we valued greatly, or the ending of a relationship, or experiencing an unwanted change in circumstances. It can feel like a part of “me” has been lost, and in extreme cases, people may feel they can’t live without specific people, things, or roles they have come to associate with their identity, saying that “I can’t live without him,” or “If I can’t be with her and the kids, I might as well die.” Can you see how attachment to an illusory self creates suffering? By identifying so closely with what is impermanent, we set ourselves up for disappointment and distress. Recognizing that our sense of self is something we create and maintain, rather than being something permanent or inherent, can help us approach challenges when they arise with greater equanimity. If this still feels unclear, don’t worry. These are complex ideas that become much clearer as you continue to develop your mindfulness practice and disentangle each thought and each body sensation from your sense of self. For now, it suffices to just observe that they arise and pass in their own right, and therefore cannot be parts of a fixed self.
faq.keywords: confusing, right, identifying, egolessness, sensations, understand, experience

faq.question: With the “Mindfulness based Interoceptive Signature Scale” (Figure 4.1) that I’m using this week to record those four characteristics, how do I fill it in if there is an issue during my work meeting tomorrow? I can’t exactly tell my boss, “Wait a minute, I have to record my sensations on this form!” How can I fill it out in a timely way?
faq.answer: Yes, this can be a little tricky sometimes, especially in situations like work meetings or driving, when it is not practical to stop and record straight away. In these cases, do your best to make a mental note of how the sensations feel in the body in terms of the four characteristics. Then, as soon as you have the opportunity, open the app and record your experience in the MISS.
faq.keywords: record, using, fill, work, characteristics, interoceptive, miss, app, record

faq.question: Do I have to record all my emotions with the MISS this week? My God … you have no idea … I will need 50 sheets like this one!
faq.answer: Ha ha! This won't be necessary with every emotion you feel. You just need to monitor the stronger emotions, because they are the ones with which you can best develop equanimity. Open the MISS in the app and fill it in as often as you can when you feel distressed.
faq.keywords: emotions, need, equanimity, necessary, stronger, emotion, monitor, miss, app, record

faq.question: How fast should I scan my body with symmetrical scanning?
faq.answer: Move at your own pace. When you encounter blank areas in the body—places where sensations aren’t noticeable—don’t interrupt your flow. Continue scanning through the whole body symmetrically to complete the cycle you started. Afterward, return to each blank spot one at a time, focusing equanimously for about 30–60 seconds on each area. If a sensation arises during this time, observe it equanimously, noticing how even blank spots can change, and then shift attention to the next blank spot and do the same. If no change occurs, don’t be disappointed or you will lose your equanimity. Instead, accept that this is not the time for it to change. Not getting the result you expect in meditation— while staying equanimous— will be of great benefit when you can’t get what you want in daily life; you will be able to remain calm and equanimous.
faq.keywords: body, scanning, symmetrically, symmetrical, sensations, noticeable, encounter

faq.question: I can’t feel some body parts symmetrically. What should I do?
faq.answer: That’s very common. Don’t expect to feel both sides perfectly symmetrically for the first few days. There will be a “lead–lag” between sides when scanning certain areas of the body, causing your attention to quickly shift back and forth from one side to the other very rapidly to catch up. This happens because sensations are easier to feel symmetrically in some areas of the body and more difficult in others. This is to be expected. As you maintain your commitment to practice for 30 minutes twice daily, it will only take a few days before new and stronger neural connections are made in the relevant brain areas and you are able to feel the whole body symmetrically, without having to flick your attention quickly from side to side. Remaining immobile and equanimous will help.
faq.keywords: symmetrically, sides, feel, body, perfectly, scanning, between

faq.question: I avoid many things due to my OCD, but anything dirty, like dusty things, is my biggest problem. Should I write a hierarchy of situations just focusing on these fears, or should I try to include a variety of different situations?
faq.answer: It’s fine to include several situations related to the same family of problems, such as two or three increasingly anxiety-provoking situations involving dust. However, it’s also important to expose yourself to a larger variety of avoided situations. The reason for this is that it’s more Table 5.5 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. C Equanimity rating Equanimity rating O Rate from 1 to 10 your Rate from 1 to 10 your R ability to stay equanimous ability to stay equanimous Morning while feeling body Evening while feeling body practice Duration Rsensations. 1 = almost practice Duration sensations. 1 = almost Day Date (circle) (minutes) never; 10 = almost always (circle) (minutes) never; 10 = almost always Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/No Wednesday Yes/No Yes/No Thursday Yes/No D Yes/No Friday Yes/No Yes/No Saturday Yes/No Yes/No Sunday Yes/No Yes/No beneficial to your self-confidence if you experience relief across a variety of fears, rather than just becoming confident enough that you no longer need to wear gloves around dust. If you recall our discussions and your experiences with mindfulness, you’re not actually avoiding the dust. You’re avoiding the unpleasant body sensations you feel when you think you’re going to be contaminated. As your brain learns that all sensations are manageable and none is a threat—and therefore there is no need to react— whatever you use for exposure in the outside world will help you progress, both in relation to contamination fears and in other areas of avoidance.
faq.keywords: situations, include, things, anxiety-provoking, increasingly, hierarchy, different

faq.question: I really can’t think of anything that I avoid. My problem is with my boss and the other staff who support him. That’s why I’ve been on sick leave for so long, but I’m not someone who avoids things—avoidance isn’t really an issue for me.
faq.answer: Sometimes, the language we use can shape how we view our situation. If you say to yourself that the cause of your three-year workers’ compensation claim is that people were rude to you and took advantage of you for so long, then the problem doesn’t seem to have anything to do with you. It can even seem like the problem lies entirely outside of your control. At the same time, we can be curious about why you waited until the situation became unbearable before addressing it. Were you perhaps avoiding conflict, embarrassment, or even your own anger or fear? You’ve also mentioned that you’ve decided not to return to that job, so why rely solely on the workers’ compensation process rather than exploring new opportunities that might suit you better? Could it be that you’re avoiding taking healthy risks? Avoidant behavior isn’t just about the things we don’t do, it can also be reflected in the choices we make. If you’re struggling to identify situations that you avoid, consider asking someone close to you, like a partner, sibling, or friend. They might offer insights into your behaviors or decision-making that you hadn’t recognized as avoidant.
faq.keywords: really, compensation, three-year, avoidance, sometimes, situation, anything

faq.question: I wonder why we should not allow ourselves to enjoy the pleasant sensations produced by best-case scenarios in the last 5 minutes of the bipolar exposure.
faq.answer: Enjoying pleasant situations as they arise in daily life is very important for our well-being, and we should enjoy pleasant life experiences! Your training teaches you to remain equanimous during meditation or bipolar exposure while feeling sensations, with a genuine willingness to experience them as they are, whether pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral. It’s not about numbing our experience. Attachment is the problem! Here’s how it works. When you enjoy a pleasant sensation, over time, you soon develop a preference for it over other ones—that’s how memory of learning works. This can lead to becoming week 5: regulat in g be hav ior to over come avoidance 199 attached to feeling pleasant experiences and disappointment when they’re not present. If you let the habit of attaching to pleasant sensations occur during bipolar exposure, you’re likely to have this in mind when you’re exposed to the unpleasant situation in real life. Consciously or subconsciously, you might hope or even expect that the experience will be easy or pleasant, or at least not as unpleasant. The bipolar exposure technique does not teach you to develop preferences. It teaches you the skill of not being so affected by either unpleasant or pleasant experiences, so that whatever the result of your in vivo exposure, you won’t be disappointed. It teaches you equanimity in real life without being detached from life. However, this can be a little confusing when the underlying rationale is not fully clear, so let’s have a closer look at it. If the exercise was for the purpose of just overcoming fears that are specific to what you avoid, then it would be technically appropriate and beneficial to enjoy pleasant sensations in the body. But the bipolar exposure method in MiCBT seeks to go far beyond this limited positive outcome. It is a method that aims to desensitize you from fear altogether—not just a fear of specific situations. This is done by cultivating a more equanimous mind, a mind that is less affected by the ups and downs of daily life. Partly for this purpose, bipolar exposure requires us to think about pleasant scenarios in order to obtain pleasant co-emerging body sensations. We then prevent any judgment and other reactions to these pleasant sensations, without clinging to them, understanding that they will pass. In daily life, this allows a more flexible way of evaluating situations and reduces our habitual likes and dislikes. This results in a better ability to enjoy situations, even when they are not to our liking. If I recall, one of your SUDS lists for exposure was avoiding social situations because you don’t believe that socializing will be satisfying, even though you are feeling lonely, right? When you imagine the best-case scenarios, for example, seeing your best friend at a party you reluctantly accepted to attend, do your best to feel the apparent pleasant sensation and be as objective as possible. Rather than liking or disliking it, try to step back and observe carefully if the predominant sensation is principally manifesting with one or more of the four characteristics you learned about (i.e., mass, motion, temperature, and density), where pleasantness and unpleasantness are not as relevant. Do your best to process the pleasant sensations only through the “sensory perception” part of the mind, which simply notices, and steer away from activating the “evaluation” part of the mind, which judges with likes and dislikes. It means that you use pleasant sensations as tools to cultivate equanimity, rather than using them “to feel good.” This is the impartial attitude toward experiences we are now needing to learn outside the standard meditation context. In 2 weeks from now, we will be starting a body-scanning method called “sweeping” to specifically decrease the habit of craving.
faq.keywords: pleasant, enjoy, life, experiences, sensations, situations, well-being

faq.question: I think I’m starting to really get the whole avoidance thing, but during bipolar exposure, I don’t understand why I need to visualize the best-case scenarios as well, since that’s not what I am avoiding. Also, can you explain why it’s necessary to visualize the best-case scenarios last rather than the worst-case ones?
faq.answer: When we face situations in real life that we’ve been avoiding, the outcomes can vary. Things tend to go well, but sometimes they don’t. There is no guarantee either way. While we often expect some degree of anxiety, we might also expect things to go well, building an expectation of success. But what happens if things don’t go as well as we hoped? The disappointment could be difficult to manage, especially if we haven’t yet learned to be with pleasant sensations with equanimity. Therefore, during bipolar exposure, we also visualize the best-case scenarios in order to allow pleasant body sensations to co-emerge while remaining equanimous toward them. With practice, the attachment to pleasant outcomes decreases, and we are less likely to feel disappointed if real-life exposure doesn’t go as expected. This also reduces any potential sense of failure and discouragement that can come from unmet expectations, allowing us to continue with the practice. As for why we visualize the best-case scenarios last, and not first, this has to do with the processing of expected outcomes in memory. Do you remember our discussion of the “recency effect” during mindfulness of breath in Week 2? Our most recent experiences tend to be remembered more readily because brain cells that produce that memory are more readily activated. This applies to bipolar exposure as well. If we visualized the worst-case scenarios last, that would be what we remembered most between bipolar exposure sessions, and we might not be as confident to perform exposure in real life. By ending bipolar exposure with the pleasant scenarios, we leave ourselves with a positive impression of the forthcoming real-life exposure, which boosts our motivation and confidence.
faq.keywords: visualize, best-case, scenarios, avoiding, well, understand, worst-case

faq.question: I wonder if I could reduce my cigarette smoking using bipolar exposure?
faq.answer: Yes, you certainly can! The key is to take it step by step. Start by identifying the contexts in which you smoke—the little things that make smoking feel like a personal ritual. You can then use bipolar exposure, followed by real-life exposure, to change up your routine, and in this way reduce craving and attachment to the things that make smoking a personal habit with which you identify. Try smoking a different brand, using your other hand to smoke, and smoking in places that aren’t your usual spots. You might also experiment with not smoking while drinking coffee or alcohol or being around other people. The idea is to make smoking feel less habitual and less enjoyable. If this makes sense to you and you are interested in giving it a trial, start with the easier changes first. In the second half of the week, break down attachment to remaining contexts, such as not smoking during the times of week 5: regulat in g be hav ior to over come avoidance 201 day you usually would. After four sessions of bipolar exposure for each context change, implementing the change in real life won’t be so daunting, especially since you are not even decreasing the number of cigarettes yet. In the second week, use bipolar and in vivo exposure to gradually reduce how much you smoke. For the first half of the week, delay smoking until the craving “signature”—the pattern of body sensations—has changed, and the intensity of unpleasant sensations has subsided. This will be facilitated by your increasing equanimity. Only then smoke a cigarette. If you think this would defeat the purpose of smoking, we would certainly agree with you. That’s exactly what you can do this week: defeat the purpose of smoking! In the second half of the second week, do bipolar and in vivo exposure to decrease the number of cigarettes by about 50%. During the third week, do exposure to smoking a maximum of two cigarettes during the times when the compulsion to smoke is the strongest— often morning and evening for most people. Then, in the last 2 days of that week, use bipolar exposure to prepare yourself for not having any cigarettes in the following (fourth) week. Make up your mind that those cigarettes in the third week will be the last for the rest of your life. Think of this as building “renunciation skills” rather than “abstinence skills.” You are choosing to quit, choosing to refuse the craving, rather than feeling deprived. This mindset shift can be very empowering. During the fourth week, apply strong determination to not react to unpleasant body sensations, just like you did in every session that you practiced in absolute immobility last week. This demonstrates the relevance of having practiced this way. In case there are other addictions you would like to let go of, the method just described can be applied to just about anything you are addicted to, including dopamine-seeking behavior, since much of addiction involves our psychological attachment to the body sensations associated with the substance or behavior.
faq.keywords: smoking, step, identifying, cigarette, certainly, exposure, contexts

faq.question: My partner keeps telling me that I avoid visiting her parents on weekends like we used to, but frankly, it’s just because I’d get angry with them and end up saying what I really think. So, I prefer to stay home and spare my partner the embarrassment. I don’t think I’m avoiding anything by not wanting to hurt her feelings, though.
faq.answer: It sounds like you care a lot about your partner’s feelings and her relationship with her parents. But why is it that you feel you can’t visit them without reacting with anger? What is it about those visits that’s hard to handle equanimously? Sometimes, we avoid situations where we might face embarrassing or invalidating comments, or where we feel disrespected or uncomfortable. In these situations, it’s the unpleasant sensations we feel that we’re really trying to avoid. Now, if the issue is best addressed through direct communication, it might be better to hold off until we get to Stage 3 of the program. That’s when we’ll dive into mindful interpersonal skills and how to handle difficult conversations effectively. So, if visiting your partner’s parents means addressing unresolved issues, then it might be best to revisit this in 2 weeks, when we start learning mindful interpersonal skills. On the other hand, if you could visit them while observing sensations associated with anger while maintaining equanimity—without needing to address the underlying issues directly—this could be valuable exposure practice for this stage.
faq.keywords: partner, feelings, parents, think, embarrassment, relationship, visiting

faq.question: How deep should I feel in the body when I sweep? Am I supposed to focus just on the surface, like on the skin, or should I also try to feel sensations deeper inside as well?
faq.answer: At this stage, don’t worry about how deep in the body you’re feeling sensations. It’s perfectly fine to only feel sensations on the surface. And if you happen to feel sensations beneath the surface, just apply the same mindfulness skills and attitude to those sensations as well. Later on, we’ll be using a technique called “transversal scanning,” which will guide Table 6.2 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. R Equanimity rating Equanimity rating Rate from 1 to 10 your Rate from 1 to 10 your ability to stay equanimous R ability to stay equanimous Morning while feeling body Evening while feeling body practice Duration sensations. 1 = almost practice Duration sensations. 1 = almost Day Date (circle) (minutes) never; 10 = almost always (circle) (minutes) never; 10 = almost always Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/No Wednesday Yes/No Yes/No Thursday Yes/No Yes/No Friday Yes/No Yes/No Saturday Yes/No Yes/No Sunday Yes/No Yes/No you in feeling more internal sensations. For now, simply allow any deeper sensations to emerge naturally, without deliberately searching for them.
faq.keywords: sensations, feel, surface, deep, body, perfectly, supposed

faq.question: It was amazing! I had an out-of-body experience. I could float around the room and go wherever I wanted. I think this is called astral traveling. Is that an okay thing to do?
faq.answer: That must have been a fascinating experience! It’s not uncommon for meditators who reach certain levels of skill to encounter unusual phenomena. However, it’s important to approach such experiences with care and understanding. This reminds me of a retreat I attended many years ago. I was amazed at the teacher’s reply when someone with a similar experience showed great excitement. The teacher said, “That’s fine, but make sure you can still feel sensations in the body and remain equanimous.” Initially, the meditator seemed a bit confused or even disappointed, but they later appreciated the importance of this advice. The key here is to approach such experiences with the right understanding. While acknowledging the uniqueness of these kinds of experiences, it’s essential to treat them as impermanent experiences that can be a potential source of suffering if you cling to them. For instance, what if this kind of experience doesn’t happen again? How disappointing! You might be inclined to chase after it and find yourself trying to have another similar experience. Focusing on the past with craving has us miss what is happening in the p resent moment and reinforces the mind’s habit of craving pleasant experiences. If it does happen again spontaneously, simply observe it with equanimity. And if it doesn’t, accept that as well. There are many more enriching moments that await you as your practice develops.
faq.keywords: experience, out-of-body, fascinating, experiences, meditators, traveling, encounter

faq.question: I’m still not clear on why I can’t just block my negative thoughts when they come up. I know you’ve explained a lot about that, but it’s still confusing for me. For example, when I do my mindfulness practice, I keep thinking about being summoned at any time for my court case. I just can’t get it out of my mind.
faq.answer: This is a common question! Most of us aren’t used to accepting thoughts as just thoughts and letting them pass, whether this is during meditation or while practicing “Right Effort,” even though research shows that suppressing thoughts is not efficient, and usually not effective [6]. Would you like to try a short exercise to see why this might be? Let’s give it a try! Lean back in your chair and close your eyes. Now, imagine a police officer knocking at your door and handing you a letter to appear in court. Take half a minute to picture this as vividly as you can… Now, switch your attention to your breath and try not to think about the event at all. You’re laughing because you’ve realized how difficult it is to stop thinking about something once it’s already in your mind. This is partly because in order to block a thought, you have to first think about it! This keeps activating the week 6: impro v in g self-conf idence 219 same brain cells, which maintains the thought rather than getting rid of it! The harder you try to block it, the more it sticks around. The key is to remain equanimous and allow those neurons to deactivate in their own time. Otherwise, the only other option is to mask the thought by distracting yourself with an even stronger thought. For example, you might think about a fun memory from your last holiday to temporarily push away thoughts about the court summons. While this might work for a moment, it’s not always practical—especially in situations where distractions are hard to find—like when you’re in bed trying to fall asleep. In these cases, unwanted thoughts often persist, leading to rumination. You may have experienced this pattern yourself! Your question also highlights an important observation: when we try to block a thought but remain unaware of the body sensations that arise with it, the thought doesn’t truly go away. Instead, it remains tied to the body sensations. Since the sensations are part and parcel of the thought, when one arises, the other does too. If we’re unaware of the sensations, it becomes harder to approach them equanimously, making the associated thoughts even more difficult to release. So, when an unwanted thought arises, try this instead: focus your full attention on the body sensations that come with it. Stay with those sensations quietly, peacefully, and equanimously. As the emotional intensity of the thought decreases, you’ll notice that the thought itself becomes less persistent. Attempting to block persisting, troubling thoughts won’t make them go away; it can even make them more persistent. But acknowledging, understanding, and accepting the associated body sensations is a more effective way to let go of these thoughts.
faq.keywords: thoughts, still, mindfulness, meditation, practicing, explained, confusing

faq.question: I found part-by-part scanning easier because I didn’t have so many thoughts. But now that I’ve started the sweeping method, I’m getting caught up in even more thoughts than in the previous 2 weeks. I think I’m going backward and losing the progress I made. What am I doing wrong? What should I do?
faq.answer: It’s understandable to feel concerned, but the good news is that this isn’t a sign of regression. Remember our earlier discussions about not getting attached to impermanent mental states? This situation is a perfect example. When our experience changes, it’s easy to feel dissatisfied if we’ve become attached to a particular way of experiencing things. This is an important reflection on impermanence—not just in meditation, but in life as a whole. Think about changes in your career or relationships; disappointment often arises because we want things to stay the way we prefer. The same principle applies here. Now, let’s address why you’re noticing more thoughts during sweeping. Hopefully, this explanation will help you let go of the idea that you’re regressing. When you practiced part-by-part body scanning, your focus was narrower and more defined. This is because you were feeling a smaller number of sensations at a time, so there were fewer sensations for thoughts to co-emerge with! With sweeping, however, you’re covering much larger areas of the body in a given period, and this naturally brings up more sensations. As a result, more thoughts are likely to co-emerge with those sensations. This is normal and is why most people experience the return of persistent, intrusive thoughts when they practice using sweeping techniques. The key is to recognize that these thoughts are not a problem. They arise because of the increased flow of sensations being observed—it’s simply a natural part of the process. Rather than feeling frustrated, focus on staying equanimous toward both the thoughts themselves and any expectations that it should be different. What a wonderful set of skills to have in daily life!
faq.keywords: thoughts, getting, understandable, part-by-part, discussions, impermanent, regression

faq.question: It’s really unlike me, but I missed my practice this morning. It’s the first time I’ve done only 13 sessions this week instead of 14. I don’t think it’s a big problem. Or is it?
faq.answer: Congratulations on being so dedicated to your practice! 13 out of 14 sessions is still an excellent commitment and remains very productive—provided your practice is accurate. In fact, you’re the only client we can recall in many years of teaching this program who has consistently practiced every session up until this week. Actually, we’re glad you didn’t practice this morning. Let me explain why. One of the key reasons you started this program was to address perfectionism and performance anxiety, which are often rooted in a fear of failure. Growing up in a strict family, you had to obey your parents to the letter for fear of the consequences. In the past, missing even a single session might have triggered stress or anxiety. But from your demeanor today, that doesn’t seem to be the case. This is wonderful progress! At the micro level of experience, your increased equanimity is reducing reactivity to body sensations associated with concerns about performance. As a result, at the macro level, your overall attitude toward achievement and how others evaluate you is also changing. This reflects meaningful change— well done! That said, it’s still true that the more consistently you practice, the more things will change for the better. After all, it’s your daily dedicated practice which has started to free you from these fears. So while missing one session isn’t a problem, it’s the continued dedication to practice that will help you sustain and deepen these changes.
faq.keywords: practice, sessions, congratulations, commitment, productive, dedicated, excellent

faq.question: I recently completed a 10-day retreat at a Vipassana meditation center and have been practicing daily since. Given that mindfulness is about gradually becoming more aware of the breath, thoughts, and body sensations in the present moment, I have a question about the “bipolar exposure” and in vivo exposure techniques. I was just wondering why you chose to include them in MiCBT when meditation alone is so beneficial. week 6: impro v in g self-conf idence 221
faq.answer: There’s no doubt that Vipassana meditation alone can address fears and reduce avoidance, especially when practiced intensively and with clear understanding. However, this process can take considerable time, particularly when avoidance behaviors are deeply ingrained or tied to clinical levels of anxiety. Here’s a way to think about it: since you began practicing Vipassana meditation, have you completely overcome your avoidant behaviors? Be honest with yourself—are there still situations or experiences you avoid because they make you anxious, even though you know it would be beneficial to face them? If the answer is yes, this highlights why exposure is included in MiCBT. For example, if someone has a fear of public speaking, they could meditate in isolation for an entire year and still feel unable to face an audience when they return. Relying solely on sitting on a cushion with closed eyes will not do the trick! To transfer the skills we cultivate in meditation to realworld contexts, particularly those where it’s more difficult to apply them, we need focused, targeted practice. In such cases, deliberate desensitization through exposure with equanimity becomes essential. Exposure helps us confront what we’ve been avoiding while practicing equanimity in real-life situations—not just during meditation. The Buddha taught this to monks in his own and most interesting ways. For example, he would ask monks who struggled with letting go of persistent sensual cravings to meditate in a cemetery and carefully observe the reality of the body, especially its impermanent, decaying nature [7]. This, indeed, is a form of exposure! This wouldn’t have been suggested if meditation alone were sufficient to address craving. If you’re interested to read more about the relevance of exposure in the Buddha’s teachings, the Bhaya-bherava Sutta, also known as the “Discourse on Fear and Terror,” [8, 9] can be easily found on the Access to Insight website. In this sutta, the Buddha recounts his experiences of confronting and overcoming fear and terror during his early meditative practices in secluded forests. Here is a passage of this teaching, slightly shortened, in case you find it interesting: “The thought occurred to me: ’What if —on recognized, designated nights such as the eighth, fourteenth, and fifteenth of the lunar fortnight —I were to stay in the sort of places that are awe-inspiring and make your hair stand on end, such as park-shrines, forest-shrines, and tree-shrines? Perhaps I would get to see that fear and terror.’ So at a later time […] I stayed in the sort of places that are awe-inspiring […] And while I was staying there a wild animal would come, or a peacock would make a twig fall, or wind would rustle the fallen leaves. The thought would occur to me: ’Is this that fear and terror coming?’ Then the thought occurred to me: ‘Why do I just keep waiting for fear? What if I, in whatever state I’m in when fear & terror come to me, were to subdue that fear & terror in that very state?’[…]” In MiCBT, we practice approaching aversive stimuli with equanimity toward the sensations they evoke, which has been shown to be more effective than standard exposure therapy alone [10]. This doesn’t conflict with Vipassana meditation. Instead, it makes good use of it! There is an even more important reason why exposure is included in MiCBT: to further increase overall levels of equanimity. This is particularly important because only a small number of people seeking therapy attend 10-day intensive Vipassana meditation retreats, where deep equanimity is typically developed. By Week 4 of MiCBT, most practitioners are able to sit for 30 minutes with strong determination—focused on the body, immobile, and equanimous. Once this level of skill is achieved, the development of equanimity naturally slows down, as we’ve already built enough equanimity to accept thoughts and sensations and let them pass more easily. However, to continue cultivating equanimity, we need to introduce more challenging contexts—just as muscles grow stronger when training with progressively heavier weights in a gym. Exposure introduces new, anxiety-provoking situations that generate just the right intensity of body sensations needed to continue developing equanimity to a greater extent. This added challenge promotes further brain reorganization and helps reduce the risk of relapse into unhelpful habits or mental health conditions. In this way, exposure not only complements Vipassana meditation but also enhances its transformative potential.
faq.keywords: meditation, vipassana, exposure, alone, understanding, considerable, mindfulness

faq.question: Can you clarify what I’m supposed to do during the 1-minute rest period during bipolar exposure—am I just supposed to focus on the breath? And when I imagine the worst-and best-case scenarios, am I supposed to focus on the four characteristics of body sensations or just on the scenarios themselves?
faq.answer: Great questions! First, during the 1-minute rest period, you just need to attend to the breath, like you did during the mindfulness of breath meditation. Calmly focusing your attention fully on breath sensations at the nostrils will help you let go of any lingering thoughts and concerns related to imagining unpleasant scenarios. Whether to focus on the four characteristics of body sensations during imagined scenarios depends on the intensity of the situation. For the most distressing items on your list— when imagining worst-and best- case scenarios—it’s beneficial to focus on the obvious characteristics of the most intense body sensations. Observe these sensations with equanimity, as remaining equanimous is the cornerstone of the desensitization process for situations that evoke strong co-emerging sensations. This practice not only facilitates desensitization but also helps reduce the emotional charge of these scenarios over time. If you were to revisit the first or second item on your exposure list now, they would likely no longer evoke intense sensations. In this case, there’s no week 6: impro v in g self-conf idence 223 need to carefully observe the characteristics of mass, motion, movement, or density—simply allow the sensations to arise and accept them as they are.
faq.keywords: breath, supposed, scenarios, minute, period, focus, rest

faq.question: I thought I’d be able to finish all five exposure tasks this week, but something came up, and it wasn’t possible to do the last situation in real life. I know we’re moving on to something else this coming week. Should I just drop this one?
faq.answer: The logistics of organizing exposures in daily life can be a little tricky sometimes. Schedules change, unexpected things happen, and some situations fall through. This is completely understandable. If possible, consider replacing that last situation with another one that causes a nearequivalent percentage of distress. If that’s not feasible, choose a new situation that is as close as possible to the same distress level as the original task and practice with that instead. Since the effort required to maintain equanimity will be nearly the same, you’ll still gain comparable benefits.
faq.keywords: something, week, life, understandable, organizing, unexpected, situations

faq.question: I had a very strange experience this week while doing the bipolar exposure. As you know, I’ve been having panic attacks just thinking about going out and being around strangers. But when I welcomed the sensations of anxiety while imagining the worstcase scenario, while I wouldn’t say I felt comfortable, I didn’t feel panicky at all. After the 2 days of bipolar exposure, I was able to go out with friends and other people I didn’t know, and I actually enjoyed it! How can you explain that?
faq.answer: Panic symptoms can be like unreliable friends—you invite them for dinner, and they don’t show up! Do you think it might have anything to do with your equanimity? Panic symptoms are often at their worst when we try to resist or suppress them. This week, however, you approached them differently. You felt equipped to face them, so you didn’t add more anxiety to your fear of socializing. Instead of fearing anxiety, you welcomed it. This was exposure too—exposure to your internal experience. Because you were able to remain equanimous with body sensations, you were more able to enjoy socializing in real life.
faq.keywords: panic, exposure, symptoms, bipolar, friends, know, didn

faq.question: My last situation on the list is my fear of contacting my daughter after 17 years of us not talking. I really understand the practice and I did well with the other four situations, but I’m not confident that I can handle this one yet, even though I know I need to because of my cancer.
faq.answer: You’ve built a strong foundation of mindfulness and equanimity, and you’ve already applied these so successfully to some very challenging situations—well done! You’ve started to build your inner refuge. Remember, intense distress only arises when craving and aversion take over, and you allow yourself to identify with the experience. By staying equanimous while not identifying with the sensations or the experience, you may still feel a mild degree of distress, but you will be able to let even that go—you’ll notice it passes much more quickly, just as it has throughout this training. Have faith in the skills you’ve developed. Make an effort to approach this exposure task with patience and, above all, be kind to yourself. Facing your fear of rejection and shame may feel daunting, but allowing yourself to approach your daughter could also offer a meaningful opportunity to reconnect.
faq.keywords: situations, well, successfully, mindfulness, challenging, contacting, understand

faq.question: When I do the sweeping en masse practice, should I just skip the parts where sweeping is not happening? I’m asking this because at times my attention seems to go directly from sweeping my arms and hands to sweeping my legs and “automatically” skips the buttocks. Should I check to see if sweeping can happen for my buttocks and genitals, or go straight to where the sweeping seems to be taking me?
faq.answer: Yes, sometimes it can feel as though your attention naturally bypasses certain parts while you’re sweeping through the body. Often, attention is more drawn to areas where the sensations are more obvious. However, it’s important not to skip over areas like the torso, genitals, or buttocks simply because they may be harder to feel at the moment. Neurologically, the most probable reason for which your attention “takes you” to specific parts is that neural networks in the somatosensory cortex and insula associated with feeling those parts are now more densely and effectively connected than those representing other body parts. Don’t take the path of least resistance. Move in the same order and teach your brain the pattern of activation that you want to establish. So, sweep from head to toes and back up to the head again two or three times without stopping to address the blank spots, and then go back to surveying any blank spots separately, part by part. Then return to sweeping through the entire body for another two or three cycles, and repeat the process of addressing blank spots separately, and so on.
faq.keywords: sweeping, attention, where, buttocks, parts, seems, automatically

faq.question: When I go back to part-by-part scanning after two or three sweepings, do I need to scan the whole body part by part or can I still sweep the parts that sweep easily? Or should I really break down the scanning for every part of the body?
faq.answer: When you go back to part- bypart scanning after two or three sweepings, focus only on the specific areas where you didn’t feel sensations as readily, or in as much detail. You don’t need to sweep through the rest of the body while doing this more targeted part-by-part scanning. Once you have addressed all the unclear areas using part-by-part scanning, then return to two or three cycles of sweeping en masse.
faq.keywords: scanning, sweep, part, sweepings, three, back, need

faq.question: Regarding the bipolar exposure method, the scenario I’m imagining often doesn’t feel “real,” in that it seems to be missing the emotional part of the experience. Is this a problem, or does it mean I am actually doing it correctly, I mean, with sufficient equanimity?
faq.answer: This is fairly common, and it can happen for several reasons. As you suggest, one possibility is that you’ve already developed a significant level of equanimity. If so, the scenario may no longer provoke noticeable emotions and their associated arousal, and you’ll likely be able to face the situation in real life with increased confidence. Another reason could be difficulty evoking emotions using imagination. Some people find it challenging to evoke strong feelings when visualizing future events, even though they may readily recall emotions associated with past experiences. If this describes you, try imagining the scenario happening in the next hour or two, which might make it feel more immediate and real. To help create these sensations, ensure you genuinely believe you will actually be facing the situation in the next few hours. It’s also possible that the situation you selected isn’t as distressing as you initially thought. For instance, if you rated your third situation as causing 50% distress but it actually only causes 35% distress, it won’t generate much arousal because you’ve already exposed and worked through your second situation and reduced your distress at that 35% level. If this is the case, choose a more challenging situation to work with. Alternatively, your brain may have learned to avoid feeling certain types of emotional discomfort, which is quite common. Visualizing a situation that could evoke these strong sensations may result in your brain inhibiting these emotional signals in the body, making it harder to feel them. If this is the case, this particular situation may need to be rated more highly on your list and addressed later, once your equanimity has grown, as the sensations associated with it are potentially much stronger than you initially realized. In the meantime, choose a different situation with a similar distress level. For example, if you had initially rated this situation as 60% distressing, but it’s actually 80%, choose another 60% situation to work with. Working with this new item will still produce the growth in equanimity you are looking for.
faq.keywords: equanimity, scenario, mean, possibility, significant, experience, sufficient

faq.question: One of the situations I’ve been avoiding is chatting with my boss at work. She always wants my attention and oversteps boundaries with everyone—she wants us to be best friends, but at the same time she’s greedy and manipulative. I will do the experiential ownership thing with her, but can I first practice it using the bipolar exposure technique for 2 days?
faq.answer: Yes, that’s often how we use it. Using bipolar exposure before practicing experiential ownership in real life is a helpful way to prepare, especially for situations you would typically avoid. During bipolar exposure, you can practice the steps of experiential ownership in imagination during both the worst-and best-case scenarios and familiarize yourself with the thoughts and sensations that might come up when you interact with her in person. This preparation can be particularly useful for complex interpersonal situations like the one you’ve described with your boss, where there are boundary issues and heightened emotional triggers. Practicing in this way can help you feel more confident and centered when facing the situation in real life.
faq.keywords: experiential, situations, ownership, exposure, bipolar, wants, using

faq.question: My practice is going really well. I can feel the free flow of energy everywhere in my body. It’s just amazing! I just have a question about remembering an experience this week that was very different to this pleasant feeling of energy. I haven’t mentioned this before, but when I was a young girl, my father had a severe mental illness, and my mother was very unhappy, always trying to please him. One night, she tried to kill herself and him by opening the gas in the kitchen. I was there, but I was so young that it didn’t even occur to me that I could have died too. There was also a gun on the table. This week, I had a flashback of that event out of the blue, even though I was not exposed to any reminders. I’m okay, but it saddened me for a short while. Is it strange that this sadness came out of nowhere, since I’ve been feeling such a strong sense of well-being?
faq.answer: I acknowledge how profoundly difficult your childhood experience was. It’s understandable that you may not have shared it before—it might have felt distant, not relevant, or simply not the right time. Thank you for trusting me by sharing this memory. While it’s often best to allow such experiences to come and go without overanalyzing them, I’d like to offer a possible explanation since unexpected flashbacks are common during practice and may arise again as you continue. As you might recall from earlier discussions, sweeping attention rapidly through the body can produce waves of sensations—such as warmth over the face, coolness in the stomach, or an overall sense of lightness. These sensations can act as memory cues for past events when similar sensations were present. Sometimes these memories are pleasant, while at other times they involve fear, sadness, or anger. It’s not unusual to feel surprised when memories you thought were longforgotten suddenly resurface. This experience is usually a consequence of good practice. Your ability to observe this memory without becoming overwhelmed reflects the equanimity you’ve been cultivating. If this or other difficult memories come up in the future, remind yourself that a thought is just a thought, a sensation is just a sensation, and together they are simply a combination—not the event itself. Neither the thought nor the sensation is part of who you are. Instead, use these experiences as an opportunity to deepen your awareness of sensations and develop your equanimity. Over time, this practice reduces the influence of past experiences on your present.
faq.keywords: experience, feeling, energy, young, week, understandable, remembering

faq.question: In my recent scanning practice, I noticed that my thumbs and pinky fingers were lifting slightly off my lap. The first two times, my hands rose about 2.5 inches after 40 minutes of practice. This morning, after 30 minutes, they were about 6 inches above my lap, with my fingers spaced out and looking cramped—yet they felt light and comfortable! My upper arms and shoulders ached, and at first, I felt scared as my heart was pounding, and I was trying very hard to remain equanimous! It’s really bizarre. I have no idea what’s happening or why. I’ve been observing this for about 3 weeks, and my palms often face upwards, with my hands bent at a 45-degree angle and my fingers pointing straight up! I am just gobsmacked at how the brain and body work; this feels profound.
faq.answer: Experiences such as these are surprisingly common and are not dangerous in any way. Many people notice involuntary movements during practice, such as fingers, hands, or feet lifting, and some even report their whole arms moving. Even experienced practitioners occasionally encounter similar movements, though they become less frequent as practice progresses. The best approach is to observe these involuntary movements and the posture of body parts with equanimity, just as you’re already doing. Simply allow the movements to arise and pass naturally, knowing that they are part of the body’s natural response to your practice. Over time, as your practice continues to evolve, you may find that these experiences naturally subside.
faq.keywords: fingers, practice, hands, lifting, minutes, inches, first

faq.question: Words like “piercingly” and “going through the body” in the audio instructions for transversal scanning bring up images of putting a big needle into my head and pulling it from front to back and back to front; it’s a bit scary when I think about it. Table 8.1 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. O Equanimity rating R Equanimity rating Rate from 1 to 10 your Rate from 1 to 10 your ability to stay equaniability to stay equani- mous while feeling body Morning Rmous while feeling body Evening sensations. 1 = almost practice Duration sensations. 1 = almost practice Duration never; 10 = almost Day Date (circle) (minutes) never; 10 = almost always (circle) (minutes) always Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/No Wednesday Yes/No Yes/No Thursday Yes/No D Yes/No Friday Yes/No Yes/No Saturday Yes/No Yes/No Sunday Yes/No Yes/No
faq.answer: Words such as “piercing” have long been used in traditional meditation instructions to convey the quality and depth of attention required, and it’s natural for these words to sometimes evoke accompanying mental images. When this occurs, it’s important to remember that when you “scan the body,” you are simply activating neural pathways in specific areas of the brain, such as the somatosensory and insular cortices. These regions interpret sensory input from your body, enabling you to perceive sensations like pressure, temperature, and movement. In other words, as you move your attention transversely through the body, you’re actually activating tiny nerve cells in these brain areas that represent sensations from different parts of your body. Rest assured, when we use terms like “scanning” or “piercing,” we’re only describing a focused engagement with these brain-based representations of bodily sensations, not a physical process occurring in the body itself. If these terms still evoke discomfort, focus on remaining equanimous with any coemerging sensations, observing them as they arise and pass without judgment or resistance.
faq.keywords: almost, practice, words, body, instructions, meditation, equanimity

faq.question: I find it more difficult to scan through the body with transversal scanning than when I sweep. And I really like the sweeping! Do I really have to use this method or can I continue with sweeping en masse?
faq.answer: It’s completely natural to prefer the sweeping method, as it can often feel quite pleasant as attention flows freely through the body. Transversal scanning, in contrast, involves going back to part- bypart scanning, which can feel more laborious and a bit like restarting from the beginning. This is an opportunity to examine the workings of attachment. Could it be that you’ve grown attached to the pleasant sensations—such as tingling or flowing—that often occur during sweeping en masse? And now, perhaps, you feel a sense of loss because those sensations are no longer there during transversal scanning? If so, this is a powerful reminder of how meditation mirrors the mind’s tendencies in daily life. By closely examining these reactions, you can gain deeper insight into how attachment ultimately creates discomfort and dissatisfaction, even during meditation. This is the story of our life! Now that you are asked to feel something else, the absence of that which had created pleasure has become the source of some frustration. Yet, every experience in life is ultimately impermanent, and sooner or later, we need to let it go. This is difficult to do, and we often end up desiring what we no longer have. This pattern of dissatisfaction is the essence of psychological suffering. Coming back to transversal scanning, consider how much you’ve already benefited from feeling and accepting sensations mostly at the surface of the body prior to starting this more advanced scanning technique. Now imagine the deeper benefits of cultivating equanimity toward sensations throughout the interior of the body as well. The benefits of preventing attachment during any scanning method will contribute to a reduction in potential dissatisfaction in daily life.
faq.keywords: sweeping, transversal, scanning, through, really, method, body

faq.question: As you know, my partner often reacts aggressively, and I’m afraid he will lose his temper and become violent when I try to be assertive with him.
faq.answer: Your concern about how your partner might react is completely valid, and it’s crucial to prioritize your safety above all else. Assertiveness cannot fix a relationship where there is ongoing abuse. If there’s a risk to your physical or emotional safety, your priority should be to seek support, ideally from a professional who can help you navigate the best course of action in a safe and supportive environment. You may also need to seriously consider whether it is in your best interest to remain in the relationship. You have the right to be and feel safe and you might need to make difficult decisions if you are not. This is also an exercise in assertiveness, but with yourself.
faq.keywords: partner, assertiveness, aggressively, relationship, completely, prioritize, assertive

faq.question: My neighbor is really frustrating me! He either complains about his arthritis or about the tree leaves from my property occasionally falling on his driveway when it’s windy. I tried to help, but he still complains about the smallest things, so I’ve started to avoid interacting with him. Isn’t it better to avoid him than risk being rude to him?
faq.answer: It’s understandable that you’re trying to avoid conflict, but this approach doesn’t seem to have resolved the situation. If he’s already complaining and reactive, it’s not the best time to assert your concerns—he’s less likely to hear you out and you may end up feeling discouraged and drained, which sounds like what has been happening. Instead, when your neighbor is expressing frustration, it’s more helpful to practice experiential ownership. Equanimity can act as a protective shield when we’re faced with negativity. It helps you recognize that his reactivity is an expression of his suffering, rather than a personal attack on you. With this new perspective, you can move away from unhelpful beliefs such as, “I can never do enough to please people,” and refocus your attention in a more constructive way. Expressing your concerns assertively on a day he is calmer will be more productive. Have you been able to use the seven-step structure of mindful assertiveness? If not, let’s think about what it could sound like: 1 Once you’ve asked for a few minutes of his time to talk, thank him and state the facts. This could be something like, “For the last four months, you’ve been bringing up a lot of things you’re unhappy about.” 2 Then state what you feel, perhaps, “Every time this happens, I feel impatient and frustrated…” 3 Now quickly say what kind of thought makes you feel this way, before your neighbor interjects with defensive statements. For example, “…because I assume that you want me to do something about your difficulties, and I end up feeling kind of responsible for helping you feel better.” 4 You can then acknowledge that your thinking might be wrong: “…But of course, this might not be accurate. I often feel overly responsible for people, so I could be misreading the situation. …” 5 Now express what you want respectfully, such as, “I would really appreciate it if you could avoid directing your complaints to me, but I do care, so if you need help, I’m happy to offer what I can when I’m able.” 6 If your neighbor responds positively, show your appreciation perhaps by saying, “I really appreciate that you understand—thank you.” 7 If he responds defensively or negatively, you might say, “Thanks for making time to listen,” and then go your separate ways. At this point, you’ve communicated your needs and intentions, and your neighbor now understands your boundaries—why you’re not willing to entertain his frequent complaints and how you prefer to handle issues moving forward.
faq.keywords: avoid, complains, understandable, occasionally, frustrating, interacting, complaining

faq.question: Can I practice experiential ownership and the seven statements of assertiveness with my children too?
faq.answer: Certainly! Your children can benefit both from your equanimity and from the clarity of your speech. You will, of course, need to adjust the seven statements to suit their age and developmental stage. The younger the child is, the simpler it needs to be, as they don’t start to develop the capacity for abstract thinking until age 10–12. And of course, since children don’t understand boundaries as well as adults, it’s important to work even more on your acceptance and compassion.
faq.keywords: statements, children, seven, assertiveness, developmental, experiential, equanimity

faq.question: When I sweep in depth, I sometimes feel very blissful, but other times I also feel some emotions I can’t explain, like sadness or maybe even anger. I’m worried I’m not doing the practice right, and I’m going backward. I’m also worried I’m going to relapse into depression again, even though I know how far I’ve come.
faq.answer: This last worry is understandable given that you’ve experienced a number of relapses in the last 20 years, but experiencing emotions during practice is not an indication that you’re going backward. In fact, it may well be that you’re helping decrease the chance of depression recurring by bringing equanimity to deeply buried emotions that have been brought up by sweeping through the entire body. In other words, it could be that the sadness is a memory of a past experience of sadness, rather than being something new. This can be difficult to determine, and it’s important to monitor, but either way, there is no need to be alarmed. Instead, during your practice, continue to observe without identifying with or reacting to it. If you do find yourself reacting, that’s okay too. One of the key skills here is recognizing that you’re reacting, and then practicing equanimity while feeling the sensations produced by that reactivity! This is what we call “meta-equanimity,” or equanimity toward reactivity itself. Let’s recap. When you feel an intense sensation, do your best to not react to it. If you find yourself reacting to intense sensations or emotions, the next step is to do your best to not react to your reaction. If you react to your reaction, do your best not to react to having reacted to your reaction, and so on! If you can manage this, then you’re still cultivating equanimity in the end, and that’s what truly matters. Keep this in mind, and you’ll find your practice continues to strengthen, helping you move forward.
faq.keywords: going, emotions, practice, backward, worried, feel, last

faq.question: You talk about self-compassion, and I’ve also read about it, but to be honest, it sounds really indulgent to me. I also can’t quite reconcile self-compassion with the idea that there is no real permanent self. Can you help clarify this for me?
faq.answer: This is a thoughtful question, and I can see why it might seem philosophically contradictory at first. I remember having similar doubts 26 years ago, wondering how being compassionate with ourselves could fit with our effort to develop egolessness. However, when we look more closely at what the self is made of, we see there is no contradiction. First, we need to understand that saying “I” or “me” is necessary for practical purposes, but it’s important to understand that these labels do not reflect a permanent, unchanging self. When we practice loving-kindness meditation and say, “May I be peaceful” or “May I be free from reactivity,” what we really mean is “May this human being be free from reactivity” or “May this human being be peaceful.” There is no permanent “Bruno” or “Andrea”; there is just this human being. The identity we hold, our sense of worth, and how we are perceived by others are all fabrications of the mind, mere beliefs we’ve been reinforcing since childhood. When we let go of identifying with the artificial construct of a separate self, we can be just another human being, and it becomes easier to offer ourselves the same compassion we freely offer to others. Self-compassion isn’t self-indulgence. By embracing this practice, we soften the boundaries between self and other, aligning our actions with a deeper sense of interconnectedness and kindness. Let’s consider this from another angle: Would you think of being compassionate toward your mother or partner as indulgent? Probably not. So, why should it be any different when we show compassion to ourselves? Are we any less deserving of kindness and care than others? Often, it comes down to the judgments we make about who deserves compassion, including ourselves. Those of us who are self-critical are often the ones who need selfcompassion the most. Our resistance to self-compassion can stem from judgments we hold about our own worthiness. Finally, it’s important to note that the mind and brain processes that generate compassion are the same, whether we are offering it to ourselves, others, pets, or even trees! The production of oxytocin in the brain during moments of compassion depends on the sense of connectedness we feel, not necessarily on who or what we are connecting with. By practicing self-compassion, we cultivate a more compassionate mind overall.
faq.keywords: self-compassion, philosophically, contradictory, compassionate, thoughtful, indulgent, reconcile

faq.question: I understand the importance of acting ethically in our life, but these five ethical challenges you mentioned sound a bit moralistic to me. Does it mean we can’t flirt or have sex if we are not married? This sounds a bit prohibitive, and I can’t see how this relates to mindfulness.
faq.answer: Not at all! MiCBT doesn’t make any moral judgments or impose specific behaviors. There is nothing wrong with flirting or having sex in ways that you choose, as long as these actions don’t increase suffering for yourself or others. Going back to the time of the Buddha, he didn’t provide detailed rules on sexual behaviors or relationships; instead, he encouraged us to assess the impact of our actions. Mindfulness helps us discern the effects of our choices and actions—if a relationship or behavior supports our well-being and reduces suffering, it is considered beneficial. So, when it comes to personal growth and our sexual life, it’s more helpful to look at our actions through the lens of cause and effect rather than trying to follow prescribed moral rules. If we act impulsively, we may miss the impact our choices have on ourselves and others. By staying mindful of the sensations that arise with these impulses, we create space to reflect, ensuring our decisions are thoughtful and rooted in care and compassion, rather than pure self-gratification. This mindful awareness lets us make choices that truly serve our well-being and the well-being of those around us.
faq.keywords: prohibitive, mindfulness, understand, importance, challenges, moralistic, ethically

faq.question: I’m not sure how flexible we can be with lies. For example, my wife is trying to lose 30 kg and she is progressing, though very slowly. Now and then, when she buys a new dress, she asks me if she looks slimmer. I usually lie to her by saying that she does, just to reassure her and encourage her. Should I really say what I truly think: “No, I don’t see any change yet?” I don’t think I would be willing to tell the truth just to be ethical.
faq.answer: The question of whether white lies are harmful is a very common and sometimes complex debate. On the whole, lying can be problematic because it jeopardizes the trust people have in one another. However, acting with compassion often requires nuance, and there are situations where compassion can justify bending the truth. For instance, imagine you’re a tech worker at a company, and a close friend confides in you that they’ve been secretly applying for jobs because they’re unhappy at work. A week later, your boss approaches you, suspecting that your friend might be considering leaving, and asks if you know anything about it. Do you tell the truth, potentially betraying your friend’s trust and affecting their position at work, or do you lie to protect their privacy, knowing it could complicate your relationship with your boss? These types of situations illustrate how complex ethics can be. We can’t be dogmatic. We need to consider what might be most helpful, or perhaps the least harmful, in the short term and long term, and weigh the potential consequences of both actions. Black-and-white thinking is not helpful. These are only guidelines. What you say to your wife would be best chosen by genuinely keeping her well-being in mind. Consider whether she might be overidentifying with her body and if that’s helpful for her emotional well-being. It could also be helpful to ask yourself whether she is trusting you to say what you think or requesting reassurance. How might she feel if you were genuine with her, remaining equanimous and trusting that she wouldn’t blame you for answering truthfully? And how would she feel if you expressed your love and unconditional acceptance of her appearance? Can you imagine yourself saying something like, “You are the same person to me, and I love you just as much, no matter how your body changes over time,” and truly meaning it? Do you think she would prefer an answer that simply reassures her to ease her anxiety, or one that reminds her she is deeply loved and accepted without conditions tied to her physical appearance?
faq.keywords: think, lies, progressing, problematic, jeopardizes, encourage, sometimes

faq.question: I really like the idea of working on my ethics and I don’t have a problem with most of it. But I come from an Irish family, and I can’t imagine that I’ll be able to stop drinking alcohol for a whole week! I don’t think it’s very realistic that I commit to abstaining entirely.
faq.answer: This is a common concern, especially when our habits are closely tied to cultural and family traditions. It’s understandable that you might feel unsure about changing something so deeply rooted. But here’s something to consider: mindfulness teaches us that habits, even those passed down through culture, are just habits. They are not fixed parts of who we are. Instead, they are patterns of behavior that can be observed, questioned, and, if necessary, changed. Take a moment to observe the thought “I can’t abstain for a week.” Recognize that this is simply a thought, not an unchangeable truth. Equanimity toward the sensations that co-emerge with thoughts helps us create space between our thoughts and our actions. You don’t have to be a prisoner of cultural expectations or personal cravings. Keep in mind that this isn’t about moral superiority or a sense of righteousness. Being able to go to bed remembering the effort we made and the good that resulted from it is enough to fuel deep satisfaction within ourselves. Remember, the point of the five ethical challenges isn’t to impose external rules. Rather, they are an invitation to examine where you might be experiencing attachment to pleasant sensations, or craving, during daily life. This awareness of craving, whether it’s related to alcohol, food, or other habits, gives you the opportunity to recognize how it influences your behavior and well-being. By bringing mindfulness to these attachments, you create the space needed to make conscious choices that support your long-term happiness and growth.
faq.keywords: family, understandable, abstaining, especially, traditions, realistic, something

faq.question: I’ve recently realized how much I’ve contributed to arguments with my husband, and how much it has impacted his depression. I feel so guilty about causing him so much pain over the years, and I can’t get my head around being compassionate toward myself after all these years of making him suffer.
faq.answer: Whereas remorse for having harmed someone is usually wholesome, guilt isn’t. Remorse shows you’re becoming more aware of the causes and effects of your actions and are motivated to make changes that reduce the likelihood of further harm. This kind of reflection is constructive and can help you move forward in a more beneficial direction. Guilt, on the other hand, often leads to a cycle of self-punishment. When we fixate on guilt, we end up focusing too much on ourselves—on how “bad” we are or on how people now see our flaws and judge us—without really addressing how to grow from the experience. When we do something we regret, along with taking corrective action, we also need to give our “self” a break! Put another way, we are so trained to perceive our faults and so untrained to perceive our good qualities. However, if you look deeper than your usual judgments and reactive habits, you will find that you are someone interesting, someone worth knowing, and someone who has a lot to offer! A key part of moving past guilt is practicing self- compassion. This doesn’t mean excusing past mistakes but rather acknowledging them, learning from them, and then allowing yourself the grace to grow. Practicing loving-kindness daily is an ideal approach to this and can help you shift from self-blame to self-understanding. Being compassionate with yourself for having created conflicts out of unawareness will also help you accept people who create or maintain conflicts in their relationships with others. Instead of judging them, you are more likely to see their behavior in terms of unawareness, too.
faq.keywords: much, remorse, years, compassionate, contributed, depression, arguments

faq.question: I love the whole MiCBT program, and it has really changed my life and that of my family, as you know. But I struggle with the concept of egolessness. It seems so Buddhist, and I’m not sure how useful this philosophy is for someone living in the West. How do I reconcile this with my own background?
faq.answer: Great question! At first glance, the Buddhist concept of egolessness might seem to clash with Western ideas of individuality and personal identity. However, in practice, it doesn’t undermine our sense of agency or our ability to act effectively in the best interests of ourselves, our families, and our communities. Instead of assuming that there is a thing or a person somewhere in our head called “the self,” insights gained through mindfulness meditation help us see that our sense of self is the product of ongoing identification with our senses, thoughts, body sensations, and habits. Once we change how we perceive, think, feel, and react, our sense of self changes too. Hence, insight into egolessness helps us see our sense of self as an ever-changing process or construct. Whether slowly or rapidly, everything passes. This is a fact of life. Why, then, should we claim that “everything in this universe changes except myself?” Are we that special? The first step is recognizing that the self exists only as a continually changing phenomenon. The next and most profound realization comes when we see that while the body and mind are in constant flux, the notion of a fixed self is a construction of the mind. Once this is realized, we feel free to live life in the way we choose, unbound by expectations determined by childhood schemas or self-imposed limitations. This realization also shifts how we view others. We begin to understand that people think, feel, and act with craving and aversion based on their imagined perception of a self—sometimes a self to defend, other times a self to hate or praise, but rarely a self to relinquish. Since you mentioned philosophy, you might appreciate this quote from Albert Einstein, a great Western scientific mind. He expressed a similar understanding without referencing Buddhist teachings: “A human being is a part of the whole called by us ‘universe,’ a part limited in time and space. He experiences himself, his thoughts and feelings as something separated from the rest, a kind of optical delusion of his consciousness. This delusion is a kind of prison for us, restricting us to our personal desires and to affection for a few persons nearest to us.”
faq.keywords: egolessness, buddhist, concept, individuality, philosophy, background, reconcile

faq.question: How do I change my password?
faq.answer: Tap More in the bottom navigation bar. Tap Settings . Tap Change Password . Enter your current password, then your new password, and confirm it. Tap Save . This option is only available if you registered with an email address and password. If you signed up with Google, Facebook, or Apple, manage your password through those services.
faq.keywords: password, change, navigation, settings, current, confirm, bottom

faq.question: How do I change my email address?
faq.answer: Changing your email address is not currently possible within the app. The only option is to delete your existing account and create a new one -- but this will permanently remove all your data including your practice diary, assessment results, and goals. If you need to update your email address, contact support@mindfulness.net.au for advice before taking any action.
faq.keywords: address, email, currently, changing, possible, existing, account

faq.question: How do I delete my account or all my data?
faq.answer: To remove data from your device: iPhone: Settings → General → iPhone Storage → MiCBT Guide → Delete App. Android: Settings → Apps → MiCBT Guide → Storage → Clear Data. Important: these steps remove data stored locally on your device. Data stored on the server (your account, diary, assessments, goals) may still be retained.
