To request full account deletion, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: settings, delete, iphone, micbt, guide, data, account

faq.question: What data does the app collect?
faq.answer: When you create an account, the MiCBT Guide collects only your: First name Last name Email address Your practice data -- including diary entries, assessment results, and goals -- is stored securely and handled in accordance with Australian privacy law. Your personal information is not disclosed to third parties for marketing or commercial purposes.
faq.keywords: data, name, including, collects, practice, collect, account

faq.question: How do I share my records with my therapist?
faq.answer: The current version of the MiCBT Guide does not have a built-in feature for directly sharing records with a therapist. In the meantime, you can share your progress by: Taking screenshots of your assessment graphs, diary, or goals. Sending them via email or your preferred platform. If you have a specific request for data export, contact support@mindfulness.net.au -- the team may be able to assist.
faq.keywords: therapist, records, share, built-in, directly, meantime, current

faq.question: My subscription isn't showing on my new device.
faq.answer: Your subscription is linked to your account, not your device, so it is still valid. Make sure you are logged in to the same account on your new device. If the subscription still does not appear, tap Restore Purchases in the app. If that does not work, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your account email.
faq.keywords: device, subscription, account, showing, linked, logged, still

faq.question: I was charged but can't access the app / my subscription isn't working.
faq.answer: Make sure you are logged in to the correct account -- the one used to purchase the subscription. Tap More > Settings to check. Try tapping Restore Purchases in the app. If the subscription is still not showing, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your account email and a description of the issue. Include the order number or receipt from the App Store or Google Play if you have it.
faq.keywords: subscription, purchase, settings, charged, working, correct, account

faq.question: What assessments are available in the app and what do they measure?
faq.answer: The app includes seven validated assessment tools: DASS-21 (Depression, Anxiety and Stress Scale) -- measures symptom levels of depression, anxiety, and stress. ES-16 (Equanimity Scale) -- measures your ability to observe experience without reacting. This is the core skill developed in MiCBT. MSES-R (Mindfulness-based Self-Efficacy Scale) -- measures confidence in applying mindfulness skills across six life domains. SWLS (Satisfaction with Life Scale) -- measures overall life satisfaction. IAI (Interoceptive Awareness Indicator) -- a body map that tracks which areas you can feel during body scanning. MISS (Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Signature Scale) -- assesses the quality and intensity of body sensations during distressing experiences. SUDS (Subjective Units of Distress Scale) -- tracks your distress levels across the five avoidance situations you are working through. ECD (Ethical Conduct Diary) -- tracks your effort to apply the five ethical challenges in daily life.
faq.keywords: depression, anxiety, assessments, assessment, available, validated, includes, DASS, DASS-21

faq.question: How do I use the DASS-21?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select DASS-21 . Read each statement and select how much it applied to you over the past week . Answer honestly -- there are no right or wrong answers. Tap Submit . Your scores are tracked over time. To view all three graphs (depression, anxiety, stress), tap the orange arrow above the graph.
faq.keywords: select, dass-, assessments, statement, honestly, applied, answer

faq.question: I can only see the depression graph in the DASS-21. How do I view the anxiety and stress graphs?
faq.answer: Tap the orange arrow above the graph. Each tap moves you to the next subscale graph. The DASS-21 tracks all three scales separately -- Depression, Anxiety, and Stress -- each with its own trend graph.
faq.keywords: graph, depression, dass-, separately, subscale, anxiety, stress

faq.question: When should I complete each assessment?
faq.answer: Each stage guide lists the suggested assessments for that stage. As a general guide: DASS-21 -- at the start of the program and at major milestones (Stages 1.1, 1.4, 2.2, 4.2). ES-16 -- regularly from Stage 1.3 onward. MSES-R -- from Stage 1.4 onward. SWLS -- at the start and end of the program, and at Stage 1.4. IAI -- after every body scanning session (or at least weekly) from Stage 1.3 onward. MISS -- after any intense emotional experience from Stage 1.3 onward. SUDS -- set up your five situations at Stage 2.1, then re-rate regularly throughout Stage 2. ECD -- daily from Stage 4.1 onward.
faq.keywords: stage, guide, assessments, assessment, milestones, suggested, complete

faq.question: What do my DASS-21 scores mean? How do I interpret them?
faq.answer: The DASS-21 produces separate scores for Depression, Anxiety, and Stress, each ranging from 0 to 42. Approximate severity ranges: Normal: Depression 0-9 | Anxiety 0-7 | Stress 0-14 Mild: Depression 10-13 | Anxiety 8-9 | Stress 15-18 Moderate: Depression 14-20 | Anxiety 10-14 | Stress 19-25 Severe: Depression 21-27 | Anxiety 15-19 | Stress 26-33 Extremely Severe: Depression 28+ | Anxiety 20+ | Stress 34+ The most important use of the DASS-21 is tracking your own trend over time -- a declining score across multiple measurements is the meaningful signal. If your scores are consistently in the moderate-to-severe range, please also seek support from a healthcare professional alongside using the app.
faq.keywords: depression, scores, dass-, approximate, interpret, produces, separate

faq.question: Is the DASS-21 a clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. The DASS-21 is a self-report screening tool , not a clinical diagnosis. It measures how you have been feeling over the past week and tracks changes over time. It cannot diagnose a mental health condition. If your scores are consistently in the moderate-to-severe range, or if you are concerned about your mental health, please consult a qualified healthcare professional.
faq.keywords: diagnosis, clinical, dass-, self-report, screening, measures, feeling

faq.question: What is the ES-16 (Equanimity Scale)?
faq.answer: The ES-16 measures equanimity -- your ability to accept your experience and not react automatically to it. It has two subscales: Experiential Acceptance -- allowing your experience to be present without fighting or suppressing it. Non-Reactivity -- not being driven to act on the experience automatically. These are related but distinct. The total ES-16 score is the most accurate measure of equanimity as a combined quality, and is the primary metric to track across the program.
faq.keywords: equanimity, automatically, experiential, experience, subscales, measures, acceptan, ES-16

faq.question: What is the difference between Experiential Acceptance and Non-Reactivity on the ES-16?
faq.answer: Acceptance and non-reactivity are related but distinct: Acceptance means not fighting or trying to suppress your experience -- you allow it to be there. Example: you feel an itch and don't try to push the sensation away. Non-reactivity means not being driven to act on the experience automatically. Example: you feel the itch but don't scratch it. You can accept a sensation without reacting to it (feel the itch, don't scratch), or not react without fully accepting (resist scratching but still wish it would go away). Full equanimity involves both -- allowing the experience to be present and not being driven to react to it.
faq.keywords: acceptance, non-reactivity, experiential, difference, experience, distinct, fighting, ES-16

faq.question: How do I use the ES-16?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select ES-16 . Read each statement and select a response indicating how much it applied to you over the past week . Answer with your first instinct -- don't spend too long on any item. Tap Submit . There are no right or wrong answers. Complete it honestly -- it is a tool for your own tracking and insight.
faq.keywords: select, assessments, indicating, statement, response, applied, answer, ES-16

faq.question: Should I focus on the Experiential Acceptance or Non-Reactivity subscale of the ES-16, or the total score?
faq.answer: Focus on the total ES-16 score . Research by Dr Alice Shires, Dr Bruno Cayoun, and the MiCBT research team shows that equanimity is a combination of both acceptance and non-reactivity, so the total score is the most accurate measure. The percentile score is also useful -- it shows how your result compares with the general population. Individual subscale scores can give useful clues -- for example, if non-reactivity is significantly lower than acceptance, it may indicate you are allowing sensations to be present but still acting on them -- but treat these as supplementary rather than primary measures.
faq.keywords: research, focus, total, score, non-reactivity, experiential, combination, ES-16

faq.question: My equanimity score (ES-16) went down. Does this mean I'm getting worse?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. A lower ES-16 score at one point can reflect several things: Increased self-awareness: As your practice deepens, you become more honest and precise in noticing how you actually react -- which can temporarily lower your reported equanimity. A challenging period: Significant life stressors can reduce equanimity temporarily without undoing your overall progress. Natural fluctuation: Scores vary from week to week and a single data point is not meaningful on its own. What matters is the trend over the whole program . Compare your current score to your Stage 1.1 baseline -- that is the meaningful comparison.
faq.keywords: score, self-awareness, necessarily, equanimity, increased, practice, getting, ES-16

faq.question: What is the ECD (Ethical Conduct Diary)?
faq.answer: The ECD tracks your effort to apply the five ethical challenges in daily life: Refraining from harm to living beings Refraining from taking what is not given Refraining from sexual misconduct Refraining from false speech Refraining from intoxicants For each entry, you record the situation, how long you managed to apply effort, and reflect on the experience. The ECD is not a tool of self-judgment -- it is a tool of awareness.
faq.keywords: refraining, ethical, challenges, conduct, tracks, effort, living, ECD

faq.question: What is a 'situation of potential harm' in the ECD?
faq.answer: A situation of potential harm is any scenario where your actions could harm yourself or someone else. Examples include: Deceiving others Taking something that isn't yours Using harsh or divisive language Sexually exploiting someone Consuming intoxicants Any other behaviour that causes suffering to yourself or others The ECD is not about self-judgment -- it is about developing awareness of your patterns and gradually applying more conscious effort.
faq.keywords: harm, situation, potential, deceiving, scenario, yourself, examples

faq.question: I'm not sure how to answer 'How long did you apply effort for?' in the ECD.
faq.answer: This question asks how long you managed to resist or reduce engagement in a potentially harmful behaviour -- not how long the situation lasted. Sometimes resistance is brief -- a few minutes before giving in to an urge. Sometimes it lasts much longer -- hours of effort before eventually acting. Sometimes you resist completely. There is no 'right' duration. The goal of the ECD is awareness and gradual progress. Even a few minutes of conscious effort represents genuine practice. Over time, you may notice that your effort duration increases as equanimity strengthens.
faq.keywords: long, potentially, engagement, behaviour, situation, question, managed

faq.question: Why do I need to track my ethics in the ECD?
faq.answer: Living more ethically reduces harm to yourself and others, and is a practical way of grounding compassion in daily life. However, despite good intentions, old habits and emotional reactions can make it genuinely difficult to act in alignment with your values. Without a tracking tool, it is easy to underestimate how often you encounter ethical challenges and overestimate how well you respond. The ECD makes these patterns visible so you can see your actual progress -- not just your intentions.
faq.keywords: compassion, ethically, practical, grounding, yourself, reduces, however

faq.question: What is the IAI (Interoceptive Awareness Indicator)?
faq.answer: Interoceptive awareness is the ability to feel sensations within the body -- just as vision is the ability to see. The IAI is a body map tool where you colour in all areas of the body where you can feel sensations during body scanning. The percentage of the body you colour is calculated and tracked over time, giving you a direct measure of how your interoceptive awareness is developing across the program.
faq.keywords: interoceptive, awareness, ability, body, sensations, indicator, within, IAI

faq.question: How do I use the IAI?
faq.answer: After completing a body scanning session, tap Assessments and select IAI . Colour in all areas of the body shape where you felt any sensations during your practice -- even very subtle sensations count. To make colouring faster, increase the brush size by sliding the brush tool (left side of the screen) to the right. When finished, tap Save . Be as accurate as possible. The app calculates the percentage of the body coloured and tracks this over time.
faq.keywords: body, assessments, completing, sensations, scanning, session, select

faq.question: Can I undo a stroke in the IAI body map?
faq.answer: The current version of the app does not have an undo function for individual strokes. However, you can clear all colouring and start again by tapping Clear . Tip: adjust the brush size before you begin -- larger brushes cover more area with each stroke, making colouring faster and reducing the need for corrections.
faq.keywords: undo, individual, colouring, function, current, version, strokes

faq.question: Colouring the IAI body shape takes too long. Can I make the brush bigger?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the brush tool on the left-hand side of the body shape and slide the dot toward the right to increase the brush size. A larger brush covers more area per stroke, making colouring significantly faster. Tap Clear if you want to start over with a new brush size.
faq.keywords: brush, shape, body, colouring, left-hand, increase, bigger

faq.question: Some of my past IAI dates have disappeared from the graph. Where did they go?
faq.answer: To keep the graph readable over time, the IAI graph displays only the first and last 6 measurements . All your results are still stored -- scroll down below the graph to see a full list of every entry. You can delete any entry by swiping it to the left and tapping the trash icon. Note: if you delete an entry from the list, it will no longer appear on the graph either.
faq.keywords: graph, measurements, disappeared, readable, displays, results, stored

faq.question: What is the MISS (Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Signature Scale)?
faq.answer: The MISS measures two aspects of body sensations during distressing experiences: Specificity -- the particular qualities of sensations, described through four elements: Mass (Earth), Temperature (Fire), Motion (Air), and Fluidity (Water). Intensity -- how strong the sensations are. You record sensations both before applying equanimity and after 30 seconds of equanimous observation. The change between the two is your key measure of progress.
faq.keywords: sensations, miss, mindfulness-based, interoceptive, distressing, experiences, specificity

faq.question: The MISS slider won't move / I can't save the MISS.
faq.answer: If the slider won't move: Try changing the angle of your finger or using a different finger. Make sure you are moving the dot along the horizontal line, not just tapping it. If the MISS won't save: Make sure all four blue dots on all sliding scales have been moved from their default position. Once the Fluidity dot is moved and the line is drawn, values cannot be changed -- tap the back arrow to re-enter if needed.
faq.keywords: slider, finger, miss, move, horizontal, different, changing

faq.question: How do I use the MISS?
faq.answer: After or during an intense experience, tap Assessments and select MISS . Using the four sliding scales (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity), rate the qualities of the body sensations you felt. Record your pre-equanimity rating -- how the sensation was at its most intense. Apply equanimity to those sensations for about 30 seconds. Record your post-equanimity rating. Make sure all four blue dots have been moved before tapping Save . The MISS will not save if any scale remains at its default position.
faq.keywords: miss, assessments, temperature, experience, fluidity, intense, sliding

faq.question: I can't see the Specificity indicators in the MISS. What are they?
faq.answer: The Specificity indicators appear when you tap or move the dot on a horizontal sliding scale -- they do not display as static labels. The indicators for each scale are: Mass: Loosest / Neutral / Densest Temperature (Temp): Coldest / Neutral / Hottest Motion: Still / Neutral / Most Movement Fluidity: Lightest / Neutral / Heaviest Try tapping the scale line itself to make the dot and its indicator appear.
faq.keywords: indicators, specificity, horizontal, sliding, display, appear, static

faq.question: How do I adjust the MISS dot positions after I've drawn the line?
faq.answer: Once the Fluidity dot is moved and the connecting line between all four dots is drawn, the individual values cannot be changed. To re-enter your values, tap the back arrow to return to the previous screen and re-enter all four ratings from scratch.
faq.keywords: drawn, line, connecting, individual, positions, fluidity, re-enter

faq.question: What do the MISS figures and graphs mean?
faq.answer: The MISS graph shows your pre- and post-equanimity ratings for each of the four sensation qualities (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity) across sessions. The most important result is the overall reduction in Intensity between your pre- and post-equanimity scores -- a larger reduction means you are successfully using mindfulness to regulate the intensity of distressing body sensations. Don't feel overwhelmed by all the numbers -- this overall Intensity change is your key metric.
faq.keywords: miss, post-equanimity, temperature, sensation, qualities, fluidity, figures

faq.question: What does the MSES-R measure?
faq.answer: The MSES-R (Mindfulness-based Self-Efficacy Scale -- Revised) measures your confidence in using mindfulness skills across six life domains: Emotion Regulation -- managing intense emotions without being overwhelmed. Equanimity -- observing experiences without reactivity. Social Skills -- engaging effectively and mindfully in social situations. Distress Tolerance -- tolerating difficult experiences without avoidance. Taking Responsibility -- recognising and owning the impact of your actions. Interpersonal Effectiveness -- communicating in ways that reflect your values. The total score across all six subscales is the most direct measure of overall mindfulness-based self-efficacy.
faq.keywords: mses-r, mindfulness-based, self-efficacy, mindfulness, confidence, measures, measure

faq.question: How do I use the MSES-R?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select MSES-R . Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today . Answer based on how confident you feel right now -- not how you think you should feel. Tap Submit . There are no right or wrong answers. Do not spend too much time on any statement.
faq.keywords: mses-r, select, assessments, indicating, statement, response, answer

faq.question: I can only see the Emotion Regulation subscale graph in the MSES-R. Are there graphs for the other subscales?
faq.answer: Yes -- the MSES-R has six subscales, each with its own graph. To navigate between them, tap the orange right arrow above the graph. To go back, tap the left arrow . The six subscales are: Emotion Regulation, Equanimity, Social Skills, Distress Tolerance, Taking Responsibility, and Interpersonal Effectiveness.
faq.keywords: graph, subscales, mses-r, regulation, subscale, navigate, emotion

faq.question: What are the six MSES-R subscales and what does each measure?
faq.answer: The MSES-R measures confidence in applying mindfulness skills across six domains: Emotion Regulation -- managing intense emotions without being overwhelmed. Equanimity -- observing experiences without reactivity. Social Skills -- engaging effectively and mindfully in social situations. Distress Tolerance -- tolerating difficult experiences without avoidance or reactive coping. Taking Responsibility -- recognising and owning the impact of your actions. Interpersonal Effectiveness -- communicating and relating to others in ways that reflect your values. The total score is the most direct measure of overall mindfulness-based self-efficacy.
faq.keywords: mses-r, mindfulness, confidence, regulation, subscales, measures, applying

faq.question: What are 'Community Norms' in the MSES-R?
faq.answer: Community Norms are the scores of the general population used as a benchmark to compare your own MSES-R results. They indicate how people without severe mental health conditions typically score on each subscale. The norms were derived from data collected from over 5,000 people internationally -- primarily from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the USA, and the UK. Seeing your score relative to these norms gives a practical sense of how your confidence in applying mindfulness compares to a broad, healthy population.
faq.keywords: community, mses-r, norms, population, benchmark, indicate, general

faq.question: Which MSES-R result should I focus on most?
faq.answer: Focus on the total MSES-R score -- it is the most direct measure of mindfulness-based self-efficacy and the primary metric to track across the program. The percentile score is also useful, as it shows how your result compares with the broader population. Individual subscale scores can reveal specific strengths and growth areas. For example, high Equanimity but lower Social Skills may indicate the formal meditation practice is progressing well while interpersonal application is still developing.
faq.keywords: mses-r, focus, mindfulness-based, self-efficacy, measure, primary, program

faq.question: What is the SUDS?
faq.answer: The SUDS (Subjective Units of Distress Scale) measures the level of distress you expect to experience in situations you have been avoiding. You rate five situations on a scale from 0 (no distress) to 100 (maximum distress). Track your progress by re-rating each situation as you work through the Bipolar Exposure technique. As equanimity develops and avoidance decreases, scores should decline over time.
faq.keywords: distress, suds, subjective, experience, situations, measures, avoiding

faq.question: How do I choose five situations for the SUDS hierarchy if I have many?
faq.answer: Make a list of 10-12 situations you typically avoid. Choose five that represent an incremental range of distress -- from least to most challenging. Example hierarchy: Going out after dark -- 20% Speaking with the cashier -- 40% Attending a social event -- 60% Presenting at work -- 80% Confronting a difficult family member -- 95% Having a range of difficulty levels means you can start with less challenging situations and work toward harder ones as your equanimity grows.
faq.keywords: situations, choose, five, incremental, challenging, hierarchy, typically, SUDS

faq.question: How do I record my avoidance progress using the SUDS?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments from the main menu and select SUDS . Select the specific situation you want to review from your list. Rate your current expected level of distress for that situation using the slider (0-100). Tap Save . Repeat for each situation as often as you like -- the more frequently you re-rate, the clearer your progress trend. The graph automatically tracks your average distress across all five situations over time.
faq.keywords: select, suds, assessments, avoidance, situation, progress, specific

faq.question: What does the SUDS graph show?
faq.answer: The SUDS graph displays your average distress score across all five situations each time you review your progress. As you face these situations and your equanimity grows, the average should decrease over time. For example, if three situations are rated 60%, 65%, and 80%, the initial average shown on the graph would be approximately 68%. Each situation also has its own individual trend, which you can view by selecting it from the list.
faq.keywords: situations, graph, suds, displays, distress, progress, average

faq.question: How do I use the SWLS?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select SWLS . Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today. Avoid comparing your life to others -- base your answers on your own internal experience. Tap Submit . There are no right or wrong answers. Answer honestly from your genuine felt sense rather than how you think you should feel.
faq.keywords: select, swls, assessments, indicating, statement, comparing, response

faq.question: How is my SWLS life satisfaction score calculated?
faq.answer: Your SWLS score is determined by comparing your answers to a large sample of people from diverse backgrounds. The scale produces a total score from 5 to 35, ranging from Extremely Dissatisfied to Extremely Satisfied relative to population norms. The app tracks your score over time so you can see how your sense of life satisfaction changes as you progress through the program.
faq.keywords: score, swls, satisfaction, backgrounds, calculated, determined, comparing

faq.question: My SWLS result doesn't seem accurate. Why?
faq.answer: The SWLS is very sensitive to how you answer. For an accurate result: Answer each question honestly , without comparing your life to others -- either those better off or worse off than you. Base your answers on your own genuine experience of your life as it is, not how you think it should be. Answer for how you genuinely feel today , not how you felt in the past or hope to feel in the future. Even small shifts in framing can significantly change your score, so consistency in how you approach the questions matters for meaningful trend tracking.
faq.keywords: accurate, result, answer, swls, sensitive, comparing, question

faq.question: What is the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: The MiCBT Guide is a mobile app that guides you step-by-step through the full Mindfulness-integrated Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (MiCBT) program, developed by Dr Bruno Cayoun. The app delivers a structured 4-stage, 10-week program combining body-based mindfulness meditation with evidence-based CBT techniques. The app includes: Guided audio meditations from Dr Bruno Cayoun Video explanations for each stage Seven validated assessment tools to track your progress A practice diary Goal tracking The app is available on iOS and Android.
faq.keywords: micbt, guide, mindfulness-integrated, step-by-step, cognitive, behaviour, through

faq.question: How do I create an account?
faq.answer: Open the app and tap Create Account on the welcome screen. Enter your first name, last name, and email address. Choose a password and tap Sign Up . Alternatively, tap Continue with Google , Continue with Facebook , or Continue with Apple to sign up using one of those accounts. Once your account is created, you can log in on any device and your data will be saved to your account.
faq.keywords: account, create, name, password, welcome, address, screen

faq.question: How do I log in to the app?
faq.answer: Open the app and tap Log in on the welcome screen. Enter your email address and password, then tap Log in . Or tap Continue with Google , Continue with Facebook , or Continue with Apple to use one of those accounts. If you have forgotten your password, tap Forgot Password on the login screen, enter your email address, and follow the reset link sent to your inbox. Check your spam folder if the email doesn't arrive within a few minutes.
faq.keywords: continue, password, welcome, address, screen, google, enter

faq.question: How do I reset my password?
faq.answer: On the login screen, tap Forgot Password . Enter the email address you registered with and tap Reset Password . Check your inbox for a reset email -- if it doesn't arrive within a few minutes, check your spam folder. Tap the link in the email, choose a new password, and log back in. Note: this option is only available if you registered with an email address and password. If you signed up with Google, Facebook, or Apple, use those services to manage your login.
faq.keywords: password, reset, email, registered, address, screen, forgot

faq.question: How do I get started with the program?
faq.answer: Tap Home in the bottom navigation bar. Tap Stage 1 (the circled number 1). Tap Step 1 -- Committing to Self-Care , then tap Start . Set your goals -- describe what you would like to work on and rate how much each difficulty affects your life. Read through the stage guide. Begin your first meditation using the audio provided. After completing a session, log it in your Practice Diary . The program runs for 10 weeks across four stages. Work through each step in order -- later stages build directly on earlier skills.
faq.keywords: navigation, committing, self-care, started, program, circled, bottom

faq.question: Should I unlock all stages or work through them in order?
faq.answer: We recommend keeping the stages locked and working through them in sequence. MiCBT is a progressive program -- each stage builds on the skills developed in the one before it. Jumping ahead can undermine the effectiveness of the training, because later practices assume you have developed the foundational skills from earlier ones. If you are curious about upcoming content, tap More and toggle Unlock All Stages to preview it -- but continue working through your current stage for your actual practice.
faq.keywords: through, stages, progressive, recommend, sequence, keeping, working

faq.question: How does the program work? What is the overall structure?
faq.answer: The MiCBT Guide delivers a 10-week, 4-stage program: Stage 1 -- Personal Stage (Weeks 1-4): Develops foundational mindfulness skills -- breath awareness, body scanning, and emotional regulation through equanimity. Stage 2 -- Interpersonal Awareness Stage (Weeks 5-6): Applies skills to overcome avoidance and build self-confidence using Bipolar Exposure. Stage 3 -- Interpersonal Stage (Weeks 7-8): Focuses on improving relationships through mindful communication and the assertiveness technique of Experiential Ownership. Stage 4 -- Compassion Stage (Weeks 9-10): Cultivates compassion for self and others through loving-kindness meditation and ethical living. Each stage includes guided meditations (twice daily), assessments, video guidance, readings, and a practice diary.
faq.keywords: stage, program, foundational, mindfulness, structure, delivers, personal

faq.question: Does the app cost money? Is it free?
faq.answer: Stage 1.1 (Week 1) is completely free, so you can start the program and experience the foundational practices before deciding to continue. To unlock the rest of the program beyond Stage 1.1, a subscription is required. For current pricing and subscription options, check the MiCBT Guide listing on the App Store (iPhone) or Google Play (Android), or visit mindfulness.net.au. For any question about your subscription, billing, or a charge, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: cost, free, money, subscription, stage 1.1, pay, price, unlock

faq.question: Is there a book that goes with the app?
faq.answer: Yes. The app is the companion to Mindfulness-integrated CBT for Well-Being and Personal Growth (Second Edition, 2026), authored by Dr Bruno Cayoun and Dr Andrea Grabovac. The book provides a comprehensive written guide to the same 10-week program -- including detailed explanations of the theory and practice, FAQs from past participants, and tables for tracking your progress. The app and book are designed to complement each other: the book offers depth and context, while the app delivers guided meditations, assessments, and practice tracking in a convenient format. The book is available from major booksellers.
faq.keywords: mindfulness-integrated, well-being, companion, personal, authored, edition, growth

faq.question: How do I access the assessments?
faq.answer: Option 1 -- from your current stage: Tap Home and select your current stage and step. Scroll to the bottom of the page and tap Assessments . Tap the assessment you would like to complete. Option 2 -- directly: Tap Assessments in the bottom navigation bar. Select the relevant tool from the list.
faq.keywords: assessments, current, stage, access, option, select, scroll

faq.question: How do I delete an assessment entry?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments in the bottom navigation bar. Select the relevant assessment. Locate the entry you want to delete. (For some assessments, tap View Progress to see all entries.) Swipe left on the entry and tap the trash icon.
faq.keywords: assessments, assessment, delete, entry, navigation, relevant, bottom

faq.question: How do I add goals?
faq.answer: Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar, or navigate to Stage 1 > Step 1 and tap Start . Describe the problem or difficulty you would like to address. Rate how much it affects your life. Tap Save . Try to set 3-5 goals that reflect your main reasons for starting the program. Goals can be specific (e.g., reduce anxiety about social situations ) or broader (e.g., feel more settled and in control ).
faq.keywords: goals, navigation, difficulty, navigate, describe, problem, address

faq.question: How do I edit, update, or mark a goal as complete?
faq.answer: To edit or update a goal: Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar. Tap the pen icon next to the goal you would like to update. Tick any progress indicators you have achieved. Tap Update to save. To mark a goal as complete: Tap the pen icon next to the goal. Tap Complete and confirm by tapping Goal Achieved? The goal will be moved to your completed list. It is normal for goals to evolve as you progress -- revisit and update them at each stage. To delete a goal: Swipe left on the goal and tap the trash icon. Note: deleting is permanent and cannot be undone. If you have achieved a goal, mark it as complete rather than deleting it -- that preserves your progress record.
faq.keywords: update, goal, edit, navigation, complete, progress, bottom

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.1 (Week 1)?
faq.answer: Practice: Mindfulness of breath -- 20 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.1 establishes the foundations: a regular practice schedule, a clear commitment to yourself, and your first experiences of formal mindfulness meditation. The core practice is breath awareness : sit comfortably, close your eyes, and rest your attention on the natural sensations of breathing -- the touch of air at the nostrils, or the rise and fall of the chest or abdomen. When the mind wanders, notice that it has wandered and gently return attention to the breath, without judgment. You also begin Progressive Muscle Relaxation (PMR) this stage: systematically tensing and releasing muscle groups to develop body awareness and the contrast between tension and relaxation. Set your goals at the start of this stage so you have a clear sense of what you are working toward.
faq.keywords: practice, stage, mindfulness, establishes, foundations, commitment, practise

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.2 (Week 2)?
faq.answer: Practice: Mindfulness of breath -- 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.2 extends breath awareness to 30 minutes and deepens attention regulation. With more time in practice, you will notice more of what the mind does -- the stories it tells, the places it wanders. The key skill being developed is recognising when attention has moved away from the breath and returning it -- again and again, without frustration. Each return is the practice. This week also introduces mindfulness of thoughts : noticing thoughts as mental events rather than facts, and not getting entangled in them. Keep your Practice Diary consistently from this stage.
faq.keywords: minutes, breath, stage, mindfulness, regulation, awareness, attention

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.3 (Week 3)?
faq.answer: Practice: Body scanning -- 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.3 introduces the body scan -- the central practice of MiCBT. Instead of resting attention on the breath, you move it systematically through the body, part by part, noticing whatever sensations are present: tingling, pressure, temperature, vibration, pain, numbness, or nothing at all. The instruction is to observe with equanimity -- not to try to change or fix sensations, but simply to notice them. This is where the Co-Emergence Model becomes tangible: you begin to see how emotions arise as body sensations, and practise not reacting to them. It is normal for some areas to feel blank (no sensation) -- interoceptive awareness develops gradually with consistent practice.
faq.keywords: practice, stage, body, introduces, attention, practise, scanning

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.4 (Week 4)?
faq.answer: Practice: Body scanning + mindful activities. Stage 1.4 begins the transfer of mindfulness skills into daily life. Alongside your formal body scanning sessions, you bring mindful awareness into everyday activities -- eating, walking, washing dishes, conversations. This is not about doing things slowly or reverently. It is about noticing what is actually happening in each moment rather than moving through life on autopilot. Immobility practice is also introduced: remaining still during body scanning even when there is an urge to move, scratch, or adjust. This develops the capacity to tolerate discomfort without reacting -- a skill that transfers directly to difficult emotions. Review your goals this stage -- several weeks in, they may need updating.
faq.keywords: scanning, stage, body, mindfulness, activities, alongside, practise

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 2.1 (Week 5)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral (symmetrical) body scanning + SUDS hierarchy work. Stage 2.1 introduces bilateral scanning -- attention moves simultaneously across both sides of the body (left and right together). You also begin structured exposure work using the SUDS hierarchy: identifying five situations you typically avoid and beginning to face them in order of increasing difficulty. The body scanning develops the equanimity needed to face avoided situations without being overwhelmed.
faq.keywords: bilateral, scanning, stage, simultaneously, symmetrical, introduces, hierarchy

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 2.2 (Week 6)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + Bipolar Exposure. Stage 2.2 focuses on self-confidence using Bipolar Exposure: a two-step imagination technique where you first vividly imagine a worst-case scenario in a feared situation (while observing body sensations with equanimity) and then shift to a best-case scenario. By developing equanimity toward both extremes, you reduce the emotional charge of feared outcomes and increase resilience. Sweeping en masse is introduced in the formal meditation: attention moves through the body in a broad, flowing wave rather than part by part.
faq.keywords: exposure, bipolar, stage, self-confidence, imagination, bilateral, technique

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 3.1 (Week 7)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + interpersonal awareness. Stage 3.1 extends mindfulness into relationships. The focus is on interpersonal awareness -- noticing how you respond in social situations, what arises in the body when you are with others, and how habitual patterns play out in interactions. Experiential Ownership (a seven-statement assertiveness framework) is introduced, helping you communicate needs and boundaries clearly and respectfully while remaining mindful of body sensations.
faq.keywords: interpersonal, awareness, stage, relationships, mindfulness, bilateral, practise

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 3.2 (Week 8)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + mindful speech. Stage 3.2 focuses on mindful communication -- how you speak, listen, and engage with others. Mindful speech practice involves noticing body sensations before and during communication: what arises when you want to say something difficult, when you feel unheard, or when a conversation goes well. Transversal scanning is introduced in the formal meditation: attention moves through the body in a front-to-back direction, developing even finer interoceptive awareness.
faq.keywords: mindful, stage, communication, bilateral, practise, practice, scanning

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 4.1 (Week 9)?
faq.answer: Practice: Loving-kindness (metta) meditation + bilateral scanning + ECD. Stage 4.1 introduces loving-kindness meditation -- deliberately cultivating goodwill toward yourself and others. After three stages of inward work, this stage turns attention outward. The practice begins with yourself (the hardest person for many), then extends to loved ones, neutral people, difficult people, and ultimately all beings. Loving-kindness can feel forced at first -- that reaction is itself worth noticing and observing with equanimity. The ECD (Ethical Challenges Diary) is also introduced this stage.
faq.keywords: loving-kindness, meditation, stage, deliberately, cultivating, introduces, bilateral

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 4.2 (Week 10)?
faq.answer: Practice: Full integration -- sweeping in depth + relapse prevention. Stage 4.2 is the culmination of the program. All practices come together: breath awareness, body scanning, bilateral scanning, loving-kindness, the assessments, and the ECD. Sweeping in depth is introduced -- the final and most refined scanning technique, where attention penetrates simultaneously through all layers of the body. This can produce strong sensations of bliss or unexpected emotions -- both are normal and should be observed with equanimity. The focus of Stage 4.2 is sustainability : reviewing your goals, comparing your final assessment scores to your Stage 1.1 baselines, and establishing a practice that continues beyond the program.
faq.keywords: stage, integration, culmination, prevention, practices, practise, practice

faq.question: The audio isn't playing or keeps stopping.
faq.answer: Work through these steps in order: Check your volume -- make sure your device volume is turned up and not set to silent. Check Do Not Disturb -- if enabled, it may be silencing the app. Check your internet connection -- the audio requires a connection to stream. Connect to Wi-Fi or mobile data. Close and reopen the app completely, then try again. Check for app updates in the App Store or Google Play . Restart your device. If the problem continues, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: volume, check, stopping, playing, through, disturb, device

faq.question: The app keeps crashing or won't load.
faq.answer: Try these steps in order: Close and reopen the app completely (force-close). Check for updates in the App Store or Google Play . Restart your device. Uninstall and reinstall the app. Your account data is stored on the server and will not be lost -- just log back in after reinstalling. If the problem continues, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your device type and operating system version.
faq.keywords: force-close, completely, crashing, updates, restart, reopen, google

faq.question: My data isn't saving.
faq.answer: If your practice logs, assessment results, or goals are not saving: Check your internet connection -- the app requires an active connection to save data. Check you are logged in -- tap More to confirm your account details. Make sure you tap Submit or Save at the end of each entry -- data is not auto-saved. Close and reopen the app and check whether the data now appears. If the problem persists, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: connection, saving, assessment, practice, internet, requires, results

faq.question: I want to stop -- the program doesn't seem to be working.
faq.answer: This feeling is very common, and it almost always passes. The question worth sitting with is: what is it that wants to stop? Sometimes a phase occurs where things feel harder before they feel easier -- particularly around Stages 1 and 2, when the novelty has worn off and the benefits are not yet obvious. Difficulty and discomfort during practice are often signs that something real is happening, not that you are failing. If something specific is making the program feel unsafe or actively harmful, please contact support or a mental health professional. Otherwise, try returning to your Stage 1.1 baseline assessment scores and comparing them to where you are now -- the data often tells a different story than how the practice feels in a hard moment.
faq.keywords: stop, sometimes, question, program, working, feeling, sitting

faq.question: I missed some sessions / I'm behind in the program. What should I do?
faq.answer: Missing some sessions is normal -- life happens. A few guidelines: If you have missed a few days, simply pick up where you left off . If you have missed a week or more, spend a few days reviewing your current stage before moving forward. Do not restart from the beginning unless you feel completely unfamiliar with the practice. The skills you have developed do not disappear with a short break -- they may feel rusty initially but return quickly. Consistency matters more than perfection: 13 out of 14 sessions is still excellent progress.
faq.keywords: sessions, missed, guidelines, program, missing, happens, behind

faq.question: I stopped practising for weeks or months. Have I lost all my progress?
faq.answer: No. The skills developed through MiCBT do not simply disappear. They may feel less accessible after a long break, but they return more quickly than they were originally learned -- like physical fitness after a break. Simply return to practice, starting with your current stage. If things feel very unfamiliar, step back one stage temporarily. Don't judge yourself for the break -- simply begin again .
faq.keywords: practising, accessible, developed, disappear, progress, stopped, through

faq.question: How do I navigate the app?
faq.answer: The app has five main sections in the bottom navigation bar: Home -- your current stage and step Diary -- your practice log Goals -- set and review your goals Assessments -- all seven tracking tools More -- settings, timers, reminders, and other options From Home , tap your current stage number, then the step you are working on. Each step contains a guide, audio instructions, and links to relevant assessments.
faq.keywords: navigation, navigate, sections, practice, current, bottom, review

faq.question: How do I log my meditation practice?
faq.answer: Option 1 -- from Home: Tap Home from the main menu. Select the relevant stage and step number you are currently on. Tap Log my practice and enter the details. Tap Submit to save. Option 2 -- from Diary: Tap Diary in the bottom navigation bar. Tap Display Log . Tap the + icon. Enter your practice details and tap Submit .
faq.keywords: practice, home, meditation, currently, relevant, option, select

faq.question: How do I view my practice history?
faq.answer: Tap Diary in the bottom navigation bar. Tap Display Log (orange button). Your log shows all recorded sessions. Switch between Week , Month , and Year views using the tabs at the top. The colour-coded calendar gives you a quick visual of your consistency.
faq.keywords: navigation, practice, recorded, sessions, history, display, between

faq.question: How do I set a meditation reminder?
faq.answer: Tap More and select My Reminders . Tap the + icon to add a new reminder. Choose the time of day. Select the day(s) of the week. Tap Add Reminder to save. You can set multiple reminders -- for example, one for a morning session and one for an evening session. To edit a reminder, select it, make your changes, and tap Modify Reminder . To delete , swipe left and tap the trash icon.
faq.keywords: reminder, select, meditation, reminders, choose, icon, time

faq.question: How do I create a meditation timer?
faq.answer: Tap More > My Timers . Tap the + icon. Enter a name for your timer. Use the slider to set the duration. Tap Save . To edit a timer, swipe right and tap the pen icon. To delete , swipe left and tap the trash icon.
faq.keywords: timer, meditation, duration, create, timers, slider, enter

faq.question: Can I look ahead to see what's in upcoming stages?
faq.answer: Yes. Tap More , then toggle the switch next to Unlock All Stages . This gives you access to all steps and stages in the app. This is useful for previewing content, but we recommend continuing to work through your current stage in sequence for your actual practice -- the program is designed to build skills progressively.
faq.keywords: stages, upcoming, toggle, switch, unlock, access, useful

faq.question: How do I avoid being interrupted by phone calls or notifications during meditation?
faq.answer: We recommend turning on Do Not Disturb mode before each session. On iPhone: Swipe down from the top right of your display to open Control Center . Long-tap the Focus or moon icon. Select Do Not Disturb . Alternatively, go to Settings > Focus > Do Not Disturb . On Android: Swipe down from the top of your screen to open Quick Settings. Tap Do Not Disturb . Remember to turn Do Not Disturb off again after your session.
faq.keywords: notifications, interrupted, meditation, recommend, turning, disturb, session

faq.question: How do I rewind or fast-forward the meditation audio?
faq.answer: Tap the fast-forward or rewind button on the audio player. Each tap moves the time by 5 seconds. Note: the slider bar displays your position in the audio but does not support dragging to skip -- use the tap buttons instead.
faq.keywords: fast-forward, rewind, audio, meditation, displays, position, seconds

faq.question: I lost my progress in the app. How do I recover it?
faq.answer: The app logs practice sessions locally. Ensure you're tapping "Log My Practice" after each session. If you've uninstalled, you may have lost data (depending on the app's backup features). Reinstall and restart from where you remember. Your skills remain in your nervous system; it's just the tracking that's lost. Going forward, enable cloud backup if available.
faq.keywords: app, data, progress, backup, recovery

faq.question: Can I skip ahead in the program?
faq.answer: Technically, yes (if the app allows). However, the progression is intentional—each week builds on prior skills. Skipping is like trying to run before walking. If you're finding a week tedious, extend it rather than skip. If you're flying through (rare), consult a facilitator before advancing. The structure serves a purpose.
faq.keywords: progression, pacing, structure, advancement

faq.question: I intellectually understand the concepts, but I don't feel them. Why?
faq.answer: Intellectual understanding is conceptual; experiential understanding is embodied. You must feel equanimity in your nervous system through repetition. This takes time (weeks to months, not days). Continue practicing even if you don't feel shifts yet. One day, you'll notice: "Wow, I used to react for an hour; that lasted 10 minutes." That's when the intellectual becomes experiential.
faq.keywords: intellectual, experiential, embodied, understanding, progression

faq.question: I'm afraid deeper practice will change me in ways I don't want.
faq.answer: Change is inevitable and, the book suggests, fundamentally good. However, your fear is valid—identity shifts can feel destabilizing. Some questions: What change frightens you specifically? Is this fear protective (good instinct) or reactive (old conditioning)? Often, the "change" is simply becoming more authentically you, which others appreciate. Trust the process; you're guided by equanimity, not recklessness.
faq.keywords: change, fear, identity, authenticity, conditioning

faq.question: I feel like a failure because I can't meditate "well."
faq.answer: The book reframes "good meditation" as showing up consistently and maintaining equanimity with whatever arises. A "bad" meditation where you're distracted but keep returning to the practice is actually good meditation. There's no perfect meditation—only honest effort. Your self-judgment is data for the practice: notice it equanimously.
faq.keywords: failure, judgment, effort, consistency, perfectionism

faq.question: I'm worried I'll become too passive or detached from meditation practice.
faq.answer: Valid concern historically raised in philosophical circles. However, MiCBT includes ethical action and the ten factors, which are active and engaged. Equanimity isn't passivity; it's clarity that allows appropriate action. The book's emphasis on ethics, assertiveness, and compassion counters this drift. Genuine MiCBT produces people who are both calm and engaged.
faq.keywords: passivity, detachment, action, engagement, ethics

faq.question: I'm in therapy. Can I combine MiCBT with my therapist's approach?
faq.answer: Absolutely. In fact, MiCBT + therapy often produces faster results than either alone. Share this framework with your therapist. MiCBT addresses the sensational and equanimous foundations; talk therapy addresses narrative and relational patterns. They're complementary. If your therapist is trained in MiCBT, even better.
faq.keywords: therapy, integration, complementary, CBT, mental health

faq.question: I practice other meditation traditions (Zen, TM, etc.). Is MiCBT compatible?
faq.answer: Broadly yes. All contemplative traditions share core principles (presence, equanimity, compassion). MiCBT is unique in its body-centered, systematic approach and integration with psychology. You can practice MiCBT as your primary framework while maintaining respect for other traditions. Some practitioners find integrating styles deepens both. However, if you're in a tradition with specific practices required, check with your teacher before combining.
faq.keywords: traditions, compatibility, integration, zen, transcendental

faq.question: I'm using medication for anxiety/depression. Should I stop now that I'm meditating?
faq.answer: No. Meditation and medication serve different functions. Medication can restore baseline neurochemistry; meditation builds resilience and skills. Many practitioners find meditation + medication ideal. Work with your prescriber—they'll monitor whether medication dose is still appropriate as your practice deepens. Never stop medication without medical guidance.
faq.keywords: medication, psychiatry, mental health, integration, prescriber

faq.question: I travel frequently for work. How do I maintain practice?
faq.answer: The portability of meditation is a strength. Pack headphones, use the app, and practice in your hotel room or quiet space. Brief sessions (20-30 min) are fine during travel. Traveling actually offers fresh environments that prevent habituation. Many practitioners find travel weeks energizing for practice because of the novelty and reduced routine pressures.
faq.keywords: travel, consistency, environment, portability, adaptation

faq.question: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD) affects my motivation. How do I stay consistent?
faq.answer: SAD is real and affects motivation. During low seasons: (1) Commit to brief practice (10 min), (2) Practice at the same time (routine stabilizes mood), (3) Pair meditation with light therapy, (4) Use the ten factors—kindness and gentleness toward yourself when energy is low, (5) Consider medication if symptoms are severe. Equanimity is especially important: "This low mood is impermanent; I'll move through it."
faq.keywords: SAD, seasonal, motivation, gentleness, impermanence

faq.question: I'm experiencing a bout of depression and meditation feels impossible. What do I do?
faq.answer: During acute depression, drop the formal practice requirements. Instead: (1) Practice mindfulness of breath only (5-10 min), (2) Use loving-kindness toward yourself, (3) Gentle body awareness if you can manage it, (4) Reach out for professional support (therapist, doctor). Depression is often a signal to increase practice, but forcing a full 45-min session when depressed may backfire. Gentleness is key.
faq.keywords: depression, professional help, gentleness, breath, support

faq.question: I'm in recovery and struggling with the "refraining from intoxicants" challenge.
faq.answer: You've already made the hardest commitment—sobriety. The five practices support this: (1) Notice the sensations that precede cravings (restlessness, loneliness, shame), (2) Practice equanimity with them in meditation, (3) Build community and meaning through the other factors, (4) Use mindfulness as your primary coping tool. The book's approach is ideal for addiction recovery because it addresses the underlying discomfort that fuels use.
faq.keywords: addiction, recovery, craving, sobriety, support, 12-step

faq.question: Meditation makes me uncomfortable, triggering urges. What do I do?
faq.answer: Start with grounding (feet, hands, breath awareness) rather than deep body scanning. Shorter sessions (5-10 min) initially. Partner meditation practice with 12-step or other recovery support. Urges are impermanent sensations—meditating with them (gently) teaches your nervous system they'll pass without acting on them. This is the core skill for sustained recovery.
faq.keywords: urges, discomfort, grounding, recovery, impermanent

faq.question: I'm elderly and approaching the end of life. How can MiCBT help?
faq.answer: The entire arc of MiCBT—particularly Stages 3-4—is about accepting impermanence and cultivating equanimity with it. Loving-kindness helps release resentments. The ten factors (especially wisdom and renunciation) support graceful aging and dying. Many elders find meditation deepens their spiritual practice, whatever their tradition. The book's final weeks align beautifully with life's final chapter.
faq.keywords: aging, end-of-life, dying, impermanence, wisdom, graceful

faq.question: My aging parent is struggling with health decline. How do I support them?
faq.answer: Introduce the concepts gently (if they're open). Equanimity with unavoidable change. Loving-kindness for their suffering. Ethical living as legacy-building. Their body is changing; acceptance reduces the secondary suffering (rage, denial) layered on top of the primary. Compassion from you is invaluable. If they practice, you're practicing together—a profound gift.
faq.keywords: elder care, health decline, acceptance, compassion, legacy

faq.question: Can I practice with my kids around? (They're always interrupting)
faq.answer: Realistically, yes, but expect fragmentation. Shorter, consistent sessions (10-15 min) are better than fighting for uninterrupted time. Alternatively: practice during their screen time or after bedtime. Some parents find dual practice works (kids do PMR or simple breathing while you meditate—they learn, you practice). It's not ideal, but it's honest and sustainable.
faq.keywords: children, family, time, interruptions, dual practice

faq.question: How can I teach my kids the values from MiCBT?
faq.answer: Model it. Kids absorb your equanimity, kindness, and patience far more from your behavior than your words. As they grow, introduce concepts age-appropriately: mindfulness of breath (age 5+), simple loving-kindness for animals (age 6+), ethical discussions (age 8+). The book's framework is universal—your authentic practice is the lesson.
faq.keywords: teaching children, modeling, values, development, authenticity

faq.question: My partner and I both do MiCBT. Can we practice together?
faq.answer: Yes. Meditating in the same room (even separately) is grounding. Joint loving-kindness toward each other (part of Week 9) can deepen intimacy. However, resist the urge to monitor each other's practice ("Are you doing it right?"). Trust each person's journey. Post-practice, sharing insights (not critiques) can strengthen connection.
faq.keywords: couples, together, relationship, intimacy, shared practice

faq.question: The assertiveness week made things tense with my partner. Will it resolve?
faq.answer: Often yes, but with time and mutual willingness. Your partner may feel threatened by changes in the dynamic. Reassure them: you're not attacking them; you're becoming clearer about your needs. Invite them to understand the framework (the book itself helps). If the tension persists, couples therapy can bridge the gap. Usually, once partners see the positive outcomes (better communication, deeper intimacy), resistance softens.
faq.keywords: assertiveness, tension, communication, couples therapy, dynamics

faq.question: I have PTSD and meditation sometimes triggers flashbacks. How do I practice safely?
faq.answer: Work with a trauma-informed therapist alongside meditation. Start with grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1 sensory awareness) before formal meditation. Practice in a safe space. Avoid the "blank slate" of meditation initially; instead, bring gentle awareness to present-moment sensations (feet on ground, sound of breath). The book's progression is designed for general populations; trauma requires modification.
faq.keywords: PTSD, flashbacks, trauma, safety, grounding, therapist

faq.question: Is Bipolar Exposure safe for someone with PTSD?
faq.answer: Potentially, but with guidance. The technique intentionally triggers sensations; in trauma, this can re-traumatize if not carefully paced. Work with a EMDR, CPT, or trauma-informed CBT therapist who can incorporate Bipolar Exposure safely. Never do this alone. The principle (equanimity with difficult sensations) is sound; the application requires expertise.
faq.keywords: bipolar exposure, PTSD, trauma-informed, professional, pacing

faq.question: I've finished the 10-week program. Do I really need to meditate forever?
faq.answer: "Forever" might feel daunting, so reframe: Do you want to stay fit? Exercise ongoing. Do you want mental fitness? Meditation ongoing. The book uses this analogy. Many practitioners find daily practice non-negotiable for sustained wellbeing, while others maintain with 3-4x weekly. The key: consistency matters more than duration. Even restarting after a break is fine.
faq.keywords: maintenance, forever, consistency, duration, frequency

faq.question: My meditation feels mechanical now. I'm not benefiting like I did initially.
faq.answer: This is habituation—a normal plateau. The solution: (1) Vary technique (alternate sweeping and transversal), (2) Change environment, (3) Reconnect with intention before each session ("Why am I practicing today?"), (4) Join a meditation group for renewed motivation, (5) Consider a retreat to deepen practice. Most importantly: notice that boredom is itself impermanence and non-self in action—meditate on that!
faq.keywords: habituation, boredom, plateau, renewal, variation

faq.question: How do I prevent relapse into old patterns (anxiety, depression) after the program?
faq.answer: The book emphasizes: daily practice + the ten maturing factors. If you notice early signs (increased irritability, sleep changes, withdrawal), increase practice frequency (back to twice daily temporarily). The ten factors (generosity, virtue, renunciation, wisdom, effort, patience, truthfulness, determination, kindness, equanimity) are preventative—actively practice them. Build a support community (meditation group, therapist, friends).
faq.keywords: relapse prevention, factors, community, early signs, support

faq.question: I'm approaching retirement/major life change. How can MiCBT help?
faq.answer: MiCBT directly addresses impermanence and adaptation. Instead of resisting change, you'll practice equanimity with it. You'll have skills for: (1) Letting go (renunciation factor), (2) Finding meaning beyond work (wisdom), (3) Managing fear sensations, (4) Building new relationships (kindness). Major transitions are actually opportunities for deepened practice.
faq.keywords: retirement, life changes, transition, impermanence, meaning

faq.question: How do I measure progress beyond the Equanimity Scale?
faq.answer: The book lists: (1) Duration of reactivity decreases, (2) Gratitude arises naturally, (3) Desire to share practice with others, (4) Behavioral goals achieved, (5) Relationships improve, (6) Sleep/appetite normalize, (7) You laugh easier. These are experiential markers. Track 1-2 that matter to you over time. Most people feel progress before seeing it on a questionnaire.
faq.keywords: progress markers, measurement, experiential, behavioral, relationships

faq.question: What are the "ten maturing factors" and why are they important?
faq.answer: Also called Pāramīs, these ten qualities support personal growth: generosity, virtue, renunciation, wisdom, effort, patience, truthfulness, determination, kindness, and equanimity. They're interdependent—cultivating one supports the others. They're preventative against relapse and essential for sustaining the benefits of meditation into daily life.
faq.keywords: paramis, factors, qualities, growth, interdependent

faq.question: What's the difference between the ten factors and the ethical precepts?
faq.answer: Precepts are about not doing (don't harm, steal, lie, etc.). The ten factors are about cultivating positive qualities (generosity, wisdom, patience, etc.). Both are important. Precepts prevent harm; factors build well-being. MiCBT integrates both. You need the foundation (precepts) before the superstructure (factors) can stand.
faq.keywords: paramis, precepts, factors, ethics, cultivation

faq.question: How do I practice generosity daily if I'm financially struggling?
faq.answer: Generosity extends beyond money. Give time (listening fully to a friend), attention (present during conversations), service (small acts of help), knowledge (sharing what you know). The book emphasizes: generosity of spirit matters more than monetary amount. Even smiling genuinely is a form of generosity. When you have resources, share. When you don't, your generous intention still counts and builds the mental habit.
faq.keywords: generosity, giving, spirit, resources, intention

faq.question: The "renunciation" factor sounds like deprivation. How is it different?
faq.answer: Renunciation isn't deprivation; it's freedom from compulsion. You're not denying pleasure; you're releasing attachment to it. You can enjoy a meal without craving the next one. Enjoy company without desperate neediness. This paradoxically increases pleasure because you're not constantly anticipating loss. The book frames it beautifully: contentment is sufficiency, not asceticism.
faq.keywords: renunciation, detachment, freedom, contentment, attachment

faq.question: How do I know if my ambition is "wholesome" or "unwholesome"?
faq.answer: Check your gut. Unwholesome ambition creates tension, competitiveness, and ego-inflation ("I'm better than others"). Wholesome ambition creates alignment, contribution, and genuine satisfaction. The book's example: raising free-range hens for profit is wholesome if motivated by care for animals and community; unwholesome if motivated by ego or greed. Meditate on your motivation—your body will tell you.
faq.keywords: ambition, wholesome, unwholesome, motivation, intention

faq.question: Which of the ten factors is most important to start with?
faq.answer: They're interdependent, but patience and effort are foundational. Patience lets you tolerate the discomfort of practice. Effort keeps you showing up. Once established, the others develop naturally. The book doesn't prescribe an order—they reinforce each other. Focus on whichever calls to you, and trust the system.
faq.keywords: factors, patience, effort, foundational, practice
